variables.name: has_lantern
variables.description: The brass lantern is in the partys hands.

variables.name: scout_ally
variables.description: Finch the caravan scout taught the party his signals.

variables.name: has_sigil
variables.description: The Redtooth shamans bone sigil, key to the wardens ways.

nodes.body: You reach Barrowmere at dusk, three days out from Millbrook with your grandmother's short sword on your hip and four coppers hidden in your boot. The market row is shutting stall by stall, and up on the wall a watchman calls the hour into the cooling air. A small crowd has gathered at the notice board under the gate lamp, reading something and not talking about it. Across the way, candles burn along the gate square wall, and a little road shrine flickers beside the arch. Past all of it, warm as a promise kept, hangs the creaking sign of the Gull and Lantern.

choices.label: Read the notice board.
Walk the market row before the stalls close.
Go straight into the Gull and Lantern.
Cross to the gate square and the candle vigil.
Stop at the little shrine of the road inside the gate.

nodes.body: The bill at the center of the board is fresh, nailed square and sealed with a heavy blob of gray wax. The autumn caravan out of Dunmere is missing, nine people and four wagons vanished somewhere off the King's Road, and the marshal is offering fifty crowns to whoever finds it and brings its people home. It is signed by Marshal Hedda herself. Beneath it hang three older bills about missing sheep, curling and unanswered. Fifty crowns is more money than your village sees in a year, and nine people is nine people.

choices.label: Ask the gate watchman about it.
Take the bill into the inn and sign on.
Admit this is beyond you and turn for home.

nodes.body: Old Colm the watchman leans on his spear as if he grew out of it. He tells you the caravan is two weeks late, that the marshal sent scouts up the drover track, and that only one of them came back, raving about red teeth painted on the waystones. The garrison cannot leave the walls under standing orders, he says, so Hedda posts coin instead, and coin brings either heroes or fools. He looks you over without hurry and nods toward the inn, whichever one you are.

choices.label: Thank him and head into the inn.

nodes.body: You fold your hands into your sleeves and take the west road home under the first stars. There is no shame in knowing your own measure, and you repeat that through every mile back to Millbrook, mostly believing it. But the creak of that inn sign follows you like an unanswered question. You will hear it all winter, every time the wind moves anything at all.

The last stalls of market row are packing up around you in the failing light. A tinker stacks crates on his cart, working fast against the dark, his name painted on the sideboard in weathered letters that read JOSSER. On the tailgate sits a squat brass lantern with a shuttered face and a fresh wick, the sort of lamp that laughs at wind and weather. You lift it, and it is heavy and honest and priced at very nearly every coin you own. Josser watches you weigh it and says nothing, which is good selling.

choices.label: Buy the brass lantern.
Buy rope and trail rations instead.
Keep your coin and listen to the crowd.
Poke through Jossers crate of oddments.

nodes.body: You count the coins into Josser's palm and try not to think about how few remain behind. He shows you the shutter trick, one flick for a blade of light and two for a flood, and where the oil reservoir hides in the base. She will burn eight hours on a single fill, he says, patting the brass like a favorite dog. The lantern hangs heavy and honest on your belt as you turn toward the inn.

choices.label: Head into the inn.

nodes.body: You trade fair for a coil of good hemp rope and a sack of waybread hard enough to hammer nails. Josser nods his approval while he knots the bundle, saying the hills eat the unprepared and always have. He climbs onto his cart, gathers the reins, and looks back down at you with something almost like worry. Mind the stones with teeth on them, he says, and clicks his patient mule away toward the gate.

choices.label: Head into the inn.

nodes.body: You keep your coin and drift along the row with your ears open. Two drovers stand by the well arguing about the caravan, the big one blaming wolves and saying so twice. The gray one waits him out, then answers quietly that wolves do not paint, and that there are fresh red teeth on the waystones up the old track. Nobody laughs. You walk on toward the inn light with those words riding your shoulder.

choices.label: Head into the inn.

nodes.body: You push straight through the inn door on an empty stomach and a full head. The warmth hits like a wall, all fire smoke and onion pie and a fiddle sawing at something old in the corner. Before your eyes adjust, a man in a red cloak is at your elbow, smiling like he practiced it, offering work that pays up front. Behind him the fiddler leans into her tune as if she disagrees.

choices.label: Hear the red cloak out.
Shrug past into the common room.
Stop by the fiddler sawing out the old lay of Harrowstone.

nodes.body: The Redcloak table is six hard grins around a captain called Vane, who wears rings on three fingers and counts your coin through your coat with his eyes. He offers porter wages, paid the moment they march, once you cover a small kit fee that he calls standard practice. One of the six winks at another, quick as a card trick nobody was meant to see. Somewhere behind you, honest dice rattle in a leather cup, and the fire pops like an opinion.

choices.label: Pay the kit fee and join them.
Back away and find your own footing.

nodes.body: You wake at dawn in the loft with your purse light and your head still full of plans. Down in the yard there is nothing at all, no Redcloaks, no wagons, no captain Vane, only Marta sweeping and shaking her head in a way that is worse than shouting. The kit fee bought you one expensive lesson. The commission rode east without you, and it is not coming back for stragglers.

Inside, the Gull and Lantern is low beams and lamplight and the good noise of people who are somewhere warm together. Marta the innkeep draws cider with one hand and settles an argument with the other. A gray little clerk sits in the corner over a ledger, watching the room the way a heron watches a stream. Beside the hearth, nailed square where nobody could miss it, hangs the same commission bill, fifty crowns for nine living people.

choices.label: Take the room in before you act.
Look in on the stable yard before the warmth.

nodes.body: You take the room in slowly, the way your grandmother taught you to read weather. By the fire a broad dwarf works an awl through a shield strap, and the two empty stools beside him say plenty about the hires he has already refused. At the long table a sharp eyed woman runs a dice game with quick, clean hands. A girl with an herb satchel reads by candlelight, and the gray clerk watches everything over his ledger. Marta's slate offers a copper for a cellar errand. It is a room absolutely full of doors.

choices.label: Go to the clerk about the commission.
Order supper and listen to the room first.
Take Martas cellar errand chalked on the slate.
Warm your hands and watch the fire crowd.
Sit with the silent drover table.
Tip Pip the pot boy for the kitchens view of things.
Ask Marta about her oldest lodger upstairs.

nodes.body: The onion pie arrives hot, and the gossip comes free. Marta leans in as she sets the plate down. There are goblins on the drover track if you believe the carters, she says, and the Redcloaks are hiring porters at wages she would blush to offer a dog. Then she adds, entirely unasked, that the dwarf by the fire is worth six of them. He has already turned two crews away tonight for wanting glory more than sense.

choices.label: Take that thought to the clerk.
Talk the commission over with the whole room.

nodes.body: The slate promises a copper for fetching up the good cask, and mentions in smaller chalk that something has been scratching down there. The trapdoor breathes cold air on your face as you descend. In the dark the scratching turns out to be rats working at a loose grate, more annoyed than sinister, and the good cask waits behind the third pillar exactly where Marta promised. On the way back your shin finds an iron hoop in the blackness, hard enough to water your eyes all the way up the ladder.

choices.label: Collect the copper and see the clerk.
Take the bruise, the copper, and the lesson to the clerk.

nodes.body: You warm your hands and read the fire crowd like a page, and before long the gray clerk is at your shoulder with his voice lowered. Since you are looking, he says, that is Bram Ironhand, who held the border shieldwall at Redwater. The dicer is Sable, who has walked every track in the Hollow Hills, mostly uninvited. The reading girl is Elowen, apprentice to old Aldous the hedge wizard. He taps his ledger meaningfully. Good crews begin with good eyes, he says, so come and sign when yours are done working.

choices.label: Go to the clerk with your reading done.

nodes.body: Pell takes your name in a careful clerk's hand, spelling it back to be certain, and lays out the terms like cutlery. Find the caravan and bring its people home alive, for fifty crowns on delivery. Then the condition, written twice and underlined once. None go alone into the Hollow Hills, so you will bring three capable companions to the east gate by dawn, or the writ passes to the Redcloaks. He blots the ink and looks up at you over the ledger, patient as arithmetic, waiting to see what you do next.

choices.label: Start with the dwarf by the fire.
Step out to the yard to think it over.
Study the survey maps Pell keeps under the lamp.
Hear Pells last word off the ledger.

nodes.body: The inn yard is quiet under cold stars, and your grandmother's short sword hangs at your hip like a borrowed opinion. You stand there a long moment between two pulls. Millbrook lies west, all safe small fields and early bedtimes, and everything about it fits you like an old boot. The notice bill hangs east of you, fifty crowns and nine people and a hill country full of painted teeth. The stars offer no advice at all, which is their way.

choices.label: Go back in and start with the dwarf.
Own your measure and take the road home.

nodes.body: You begin the long walk home by lantern light, leaving the fifty crowns for harder hands. It is a sensible decision, made soberly. Nobody in Millbrook ever faults you for it. But years from now, mending fences on a spring morning, you will still catch yourself wondering who finally brought the drovers home, or whether anyone ever did.

Bram Ironhand works his awl through a shield strap by the fire, border scars on his forearms and a mug gone cold at his elbow. He has turned away two hires tonight already, without once looking up from the leather. The awl bites and the strap turns. The silence around him is a moat, and other people's confidence has drowned in it twice this evening. You consider your approach the way you would consider river ice.

choices.label: Offer coin, loud enough for the room.
Speak plainly of the missing drovers and their families.
Say nothing and hold the strap steady for his awl.
Buy him a proper ale and wait him out.

nodes.body: You make your offer loud enough for the room, and the dwarf's eyes come up flat as slate. Coin talk is cheap talk, he says, since every fool jingles. But then he sets the awl down and asks the one question that apparently matters, the one the other hires failed. What will you do up there, he asks quietly, when it turns bad and running looks wise. The fire pops while he waits, and you understand the answer is the whole interview.

choices.label: Answer honest, afraid and going anyway.
Boast that you fear nothing.

nodes.body: You speak plainly, no flourish. Nine drovers and a merchant missing, and in Dunmere tonight there are children lighting candles in windows for parents who are two weeks late. The telling lands somewhere true, because the awl stops. Then Bram asks the one question that matters, in a voice like a door being tested. What will you do up there when it turns bad, he says, and running looks wise. You understand that everything hangs on the answer.

choices.label: Answer honest, afraid and going anyway.
Swear you would never run from anything.

nodes.body: You say nothing at all. You simply kneel and hold the strap steady so the awl can bite true, and after a while there is a grunt that might be thanks in some border dialect. Three rivets later he speaks without looking up, low, as if asking the leather. What will you do up there, he says, when it turns bad and running looks wise. The question hangs in the fire heat, and you understand it is the only test he owns.

choices.label: Answer honest, afraid and going anyway.
Claim your nerve has never broken.

nodes.body: Bram snorts like a bellows and turns back to his strap for good. I have dug graves for a dozen fearless men, he says to the leather, and none of them helped me dig. Word moves through the room the way water moves through straw, and by midnight no shield in Barrowmere will sign under you. At dawn the writ passes quietly to the Redcloaks, and the east road opens for somebody else.

The awl goes down at last. The dwarf reaches under the bench and comes up with an axe whose haft is worn to the exact shape of his hands, and he says only that Ironhand comes. No terms and no speeches, just a border veteran standing up while the room quietly recalculates your chances. Then he nods across at the dice table and advises you to see to the dicer next, if you have sense.

choices.label: Head for the dice table.

nodes.body: Sable deals dice with quick, clean hands, her hair cropped short and three knives visible, which by rumor means at least three more are not. She invites you to sit with a grin that weighs you like a market scale, worth on one side and trouble on the other. The players around her are losing steadily and enjoying it, which tells you something about her talent. Her eyes flick once to the sword at your hip, then back to your face, where the real reading happens.

choices.label: Play a few honest rounds with your last coins.
Sit but watch her hands instead of the dice.
Skip the game and offer the job flat out.
Stake your grandmothers sword on one roll.

nodes.body: You play three small rounds with your last honest coins and lose every one, and at the third loss you laugh, because the trick of it is genuinely beautiful. That surprises her more than winning would have done. She stacks the dice, studies you, and names her price without ceremony. Not coin, she says, but proof you can keep your head. Tell her the odds of this venture exactly as you reckon them, and she will know if you dress them up.

choices.label: Tell her the odds, bad and getting worse.
Dress the odds up pretty to close the deal.

nodes.body: You sit, but you watch her hands instead of the dice, the way your grandmother watched horse traders. It takes four rounds to catch it, a weighted die walking between her fingers smooth as water over a stone. Then her eyes meet yours, and she knows you caught it, and the whole table hangs there waiting on what you do with the knowledge. Her grin does not waver, but it sharpens at the edges, and one hand drifts toward the coin pile, ready either way.

choices.label: Say it quietly, just for her.
Call the cheat for the whole room.

nodes.body: The table erupts like a kicked anthill. A drover grabs Sable's collar, mugs go over, and somebody's supper becomes a weapon of opportunity. You back away into the cold yard while the noise sorts itself out, and a moment later Sable drops from the woodshed roof, laughing and entirely unbothered. You started her favorite kind of evening, she says, and never even swung, which shows a rare cool head. She offers her hand on an equal cut, still grinning.

choices.label: Shake on it and rest before finding the wizard.
Wade back inside to settle the drover.

nodes.body: The watch wades in with staves before the second mug lands, and they are not selective about who they collect. You spend the night in the lockup, which smells of turnips and regret in about equal measure. In the gray morning, Pell appears at the bars with an apologetic shrug and signs the writ over to the Redcloaks where you can watch him do it. The lesson costs more than the stool did.

You skip the game entirely and lay the job out flat. Sable spins a knife on its point against the tabletop, bored in a practiced way, and asks why she would walk into goblin country for a farmhand's handshake and a marshal's coin. Then she names her actual price, and it is not silver. Tell her the odds exactly as you reckon them, she says, with no dressing on them, and she will decide whether your head is worth following into the hills.

choices.label: Tell her the odds, bad and getting worse.
Promise her glory and easy silver.

nodes.body: You dress the odds up pretty, and she watches you do it the way a fishmonger watches a thumb on the scale. She flips her knife home and stands. Pretty odds weigh more than packs in the hills, she says, and some door takes her that nobody saw open. Without a scout, Pell cannot in conscience send you east, and by morning he does not have to.

The truth lands like a fair trade. Bad odds, you say, and getting worse with every day the trail cools, which is exactly why the job matters to anybody worth hiring. She considers this, then nods once and names her terms, an equal cut and first pick of any boots they find. Then she tips her chin toward the empty chair by the candle. You will want a wizard for those stones, she says, and she happens to know where that one sleeps.

choices.label: Take a nights rest before finding the wizard.

nodes.body: You climb to a straw bed in the loft with the whole plan buzzing in your skull. Below you Bram snores like a forge in steady labor, and somewhere a shutter taps. Plans and fears trade watches in your head for an hour, maybe two, until sleep finally takes the field and holds it. You wake at first light feeling stronger than you have since Millbrook, which is worth more than coin where you are going.

choices.label: Rise at first light.

nodes.body: The herb yard gate stands open at first light. The argument drifting over it is polite and completely immovable on both sides. Elowen stands with her satchel already packed while old Master Aldous tells her the Hollow Hills have killed better wizards than half trained girls. She agrees with him pleasantly and does not unpack. Then they both turn and look at you at the same moment, as if you had walked in carrying the deciding vote and everybody knew it.

choices.label: Vouch for her to Master Aldous.
Offer to fetch her unfinished errand first, stormroot from the hedgerows.
Decide to go without a wizard.
Ask Elowen herself what she wants.

nodes.body: You step up and vouch for her, and Aldous turns his milky eyes on you with unsettling accuracy. He asks one question, in the voice of a man who has buried students. What will you do, he says, on the day my apprentice stands in over her head with her three small spells spent and the dark still coming. The herb yard goes very quiet, and you can hear Elowen not breathing while she waits on your answer.

choices.label: Swear to send her home at first true danger.
Promise him glory and a tale for the guild.

nodes.body: Your answer comes out shaped like a tavern song, and the gate shuts with a hedge wizard's finality, which is quieter than a slam and far more permanent. Elowen mouths sorry to you through the slats. At the east gate, Hedda counts three where the writ requires four and refuses to sign living people into warded country without craft beside them. By noon the writ is gone, and so are the Redcloaks.

The hedgerows are silver with dew. The stormroot grows exactly where Elowen calls directions from the gate, down in the ditch shade with its thorned runners crossed like a warning it learned somewhere. You cut the runner clean at the joint the way she instructs, and you carry it back whole and unbruised. Aldous weighs the root in his palm for a long moment. You understand that he is actually weighing the care it was carried with.

choices.label: Hand it over and let the root argue for you.
Hand it over and add your promise to send her home at true danger.

nodes.body: You walk back toward the east gate telling yourself that steel and sense will do, that plenty of crews manage without craft, that wizards are for stories. It is a reasonable speech and you give it well. But the waystone rumors keep turning over in your head, and under them, in Maren's dry voice and the fiddler's tune and the porter's tale, one word keeps surfacing like a fish. Warded. Warded by whom, and against what, exactly.

choices.label: Turn back and give Aldous your oath to send her home at true danger.
Report to the east gate with three of you.

nodes.body: Marshal Hedda counts your crew at the east gate and arrives at three, where the writ plainly requires four. She looks at you for a long moment, not unkindly, then rolls the writ closed. I do not sign living people into warded country without craft beside them, she says. You watch from the wall as the Redcloaks take the road east, making noise enough to warn every crow in the county.

Aldous presses a stick of warding chalk into Elowen's hand and closes her fingers over it with both of his. Draw your circles small, he tells her, and come home. She shoulders her satchel, three true spells and endless questions and all, and joins you in the lane where Bram and Sable wait. Four of you now, a crew by any honest count, walking toward the east gate in the young light.

choices.label: Report to Marshal Hedda at the east gate.

nodes.body: Marshal Hedda meets you at the east gate at dawn, gray as gate iron, and reads your faces like a muster roll. The terms once more, spoken plain. Find the caravan, bring its people home alive. She presses the road writ into your hand while Pell inks your crew into the ledger as the Company of the Gull and Lantern, which the whole gatehouse shortens to the Brass Lantern before the wax is even dry. Beyond the arch the King's Road runs east into mist, and Josser calls one last time from his cart.

choices.label: Trade your spare kit for Jossers brass lantern and march.
Take the road as you are.
Hear Heddas last word on the hills before you go.
Ask what head start the Redcloaks have.
Take your leave of the vigil grandmother at the gate.

nodes.body: Hedda refuses you a cart flatly, and riders along with it. The roads east are torn by autumn rain and by worse than rain, she explains, and a wheel caught in a rut out there is a dinner bell. Her parting word follows you under the archway, dry as gravel. Whatever painted those stones counts wheels as easy meat and walkers as slow meat, so walk fast.

choices.label: Take the road on foot.

nodes.body: A mile east of town the ways part at a leaning milestone, and the company halts around it. The high road runs open and fast along the ridges, pale in the morning sun. Below in the valley a barge horn sounds from the river landing, where the eel weirs stitch the water. And straight ahead the old drover track cuts rough and honest toward the rumors, past a shrine hollow and a thread of smoke that should not be there. Sable chews a grass stem and waits on your word, patient as the milestone.

choices.label: Take the high road along the ridges.
Ride the river barge from the landing.
Walk the old drover track.
Check the smoke rising off the second farmstead first.
Work the eel weirs below the landing for the shortcut.
Pay respects at the drover shrine in the hollow.

nodes.body: The high road runs white and open under the morning sun, and it is wrong in a way that takes a moment to name. No carts. Weeds standing in the wheel ruts. Bram reads the emptiness like a bad ledger, saying nothing and gripping his axe strap. Far ahead a dust plume hangs over the road, riders maybe, or something that wants to look like riders. The hedges on either side offer their shadows, and the fallow fields spread away south, tangled and gold.

choices.label: March to meet the riders openly.
Slip into the hedge and watch them pass.
Cut away from the road through the fallow fields at once.
Detour by the toll shrine keeper first.
Take the shepherds shortcut over the downs.

nodes.body: The riders prove to be a king's patrol, and their sergeant reins in with the gray face of a man short on sleep. The garrison rides no farther east than the toll shrine, he tells you, under orders he clearly despises. Crows have been thick over the fallow country for two days now, and red teeth on the waystones means the Redtooth band, goblins who count and plan, which is considerably worse than goblins who howl. His advice comes plain and free. Turn back while the turning is good.

choices.label: Push on to the toll shrine.
Trade him tobacco for a look at his road map.

nodes.body: The hedge hides you from the riders well enough, but not from the three ragged men already using it. Deserters, by the half uniforms and the way they stand, and the nearest taps a rusty blade against his palm while he names a toll for walking the king's own road. His friends spread out a little, casual as weather, cutting the angles back to the open field. It is a small ugly moment with several ways out of it, none of them free.

choices.label: Pay a little and pass.
Let Bram loom and talk them down, soldier to almost soldier.
Refuse and reach for iron.

nodes.body: Iron answers iron, and then numbers answer both. You take a gash across your sword arm before Bram can reach you, and the party drags you west while the deserters whoop from their hedge like carrion birds. The wound closes slowly through the gray end of autumn, and by the time you can grip a hilt again, the trail is cold and the season is shut.

You cut away from the road at once, straight across the fallow country, and the old furrows grab at your ankles like the fields resent company. The road dwindles behind. Before noon, wolves show themselves at the field edge, five gray shapes keeping pace with you, unhurried and bold with winter coming on. They do not charge and they do not leave. They simply walk your flank like an escort you never hired, waiting to see what you are made of.

choices.label: Stand together and drive them back.
Throw the ration sack and run for the shrine wall.

nodes.body: The thrown ration sack buys you perhaps twenty heartbeats, and the wolves spend them well. They run you down in the third field, all teeth and shoulder weight, and it is only your companions hauling you back over the toll shrine wall that keeps you alive, torn but breathing. The wounds knit at Barrowmere under Marta's roof. The commission does not wait for bandages, and neither does the season.

The toll shrine stands at the road's high bend, and it has been robbed with a thoroughness that feels personal. Its little bell is stolen off the yoke, its alms box pried open and licked clean. But the steps still hold, and from them the whole fallow country spreads out below, gold and tangled and quiet. Over the second fallow, wheeling in a slow black funnel that never quite settles, the crows are keeping some appointment of their own.

choices.label: Make for the crows.
Dig the stolen bell out of the ditch and rehang it.

nodes.body: The sergeant's map buys you the farmstead shortcut, and the farmstead buys you a lesson. Its door stands open on a kitchen where porridge has dried in the pot and nobody came back for it. Inside the door frame, scratched fresh over four little tally strokes, grins a red tooth. Elowen touches the marks and counts aloud, four, and does not say four what. Beyond the yard the crow funnel turns patiently over the fallow country.

choices.label: Take the shortcut to the crow fields.

nodes.body: You stand together, and it becomes shields and shouting and the sharp gray smell of wolf. Bram's axe haft cracks a skull sideways hard enough to turn the argument, and the pack decides all at once that you cost too much, flowing away north like spilled water finding a drain. You bind the scratches in the lee of a wall. Miles remain, and the day is spending itself faster than you would like.

choices.label: Bind up and make for the crows.

nodes.body: The river landing smells of tar and eel and cold water, and only one barge is still working this late in the season. Its master is a knuckled old man named Weck, who looks your company over from under an oiled hat and names a fare in the voice of a man reading weather aloud. He does not haggle. It becomes clear within a sentence that he has never haggled in his life, and that the river respects him for it. Downstream, his grandnephew mends eel traps and whistles.

choices.label: Pay the fare and ride as cargo.
Work the pole and lines for passage.
Pay the eel boy on the landing for river news.

nodes.body: You pay the fare and ride as cargo among the onion sacks, and Weck talks the way rivers move, slow and certain and mostly one direction. The caravan never reached Dunmere landing, he says, and no wreck came down the current either, which means the road took it, not the water. Then, older talk. Harrowstone Keep above the Hollow Hills, king built and plague emptied, has been burning green light at wrong hours lately. Goblins do not make green fire, he says. Scholars do.

choices.label: Watch the fog bank rolling in ahead.
Help Weck sound the channel as the fog takes you.
Lean out and study the strange wake in the fog.

nodes.body: You work your passage honest, pole and lines, rope burning your palms while Weck grunts approval and loosens by about a knot an hour. At dusk wolves come down to the bank and watch the barge slide past like a moving fort. Nobody aboard says anything clever about it. Then fog begins to rise off the water with the dark, thick and white and quiet, and Weck's hand finds the tiller without his eyes going anywhere at all.

choices.label: Stand ready at the rail through the fog.
Sound the channel with the pole where Weck points.
Accept the charm Weck presses on Elowen at the rail.

nodes.body: Shapes move in the fog, and then one of them does not move. A snagged tree takes the hull a glancing blow that tilts the whole world sideways, onion sacks going over the rail while everyone grabs for rope and each other. Weck wrestles her level and puts in at Dunmere landing with a hole to patch. At the waterline, families are already gathering, asking after the caravan by name, one name at a time, in voices that have practiced not hoping.

choices.label: Promise them word and follow the crows west.
Jump for the shallows when the hull hits.

nodes.body: The shallows are deeper than they look, and the current is colder and stronger than any argument you brought with you. Bram drags you out coughing a mile downstream, minus your pack, your bedroll, and very nearly the rest of it. The river keeps the kit and the season both. There is nothing left for it but the long walk home, wet, and the sound of the water laughing all the way.

Your pole finds the snag a breath before the hull does, and Weck heaves the tiller past a drowned oak with a foot to spare, swearing gently in fisherman's Latin. Dunmere landing arrives at dawn. At the waterline a mother stands holding a carved toy horse, and she tells you what the manifests will not. Merchant Tallow carried a sealed crate for the abbey at Northgate, a reliquary, freight paid double for haste and guards. The kind of cargo somebody plans around.

choices.label: Promise her word and follow the crows west.
Walk the last west bank stretch reading the mud.

nodes.body: The old drover track is turf worn down to bone by generations of herds, running straight at the hills without apology. The first waystone leans like a bad tooth, and painted across its face in rust red grins a row of teeth, fresh enough to shine. Sable is already on one knee beside the track, reading the ground the way Pell reads ledgers. On the rise ahead, something small stands watching the company, and is gone between one blink and the next.

choices.label: Give her the hour to read it right.
Push the pace, reading can wait.
Hail the small figure watching from the rise.
Test the drover tables trick, count the teeth.

nodes.body: You give Sable her hour, and the ground pays it back with interest. Many goblin feet moving east, she says, in order, not raiding. Laden wheel ruts a week old, and over them, days fresher, boot prints dragging the way people walk when they are driven. Her face has gone flat and hard by the end of it. Then comes the moor crossing, black pools between pale tussocks, and Elowen walks ahead calling the safe steps aloud like a schoolroom lesson nobody dares fail.

choices.label: Step only where she counts.
Trust your own eye and take the straighter line.

nodes.body: You push the pace, because reading can wait and the missing cannot. It works for six fast miles, right up until Elowen pulls the whole company flat behind the bank with one yank of her arm. A snare line of sinew runs across the track at ankle height, strung clever and taut, nearly invisible against the turf. Past it, now that you are looking, there are more. The track itself has gone hostile, and it happened somewhere in the last mile without announcing itself.

choices.label: Pick through slow and eat the lost time.
Keep the hard pace and sharp eyes.

nodes.body: Your hail rolls out over the moor and the moor swallows it whole. Then comes the answer, one horn note, far off, flat and wrong in a way that raises the hair on your arms. Sable turns on you with a look that could skin a rabbit at forty paces. On the ridgeline, small shapes have begun to trot in files, purposeful now, and the empty country between you and them suddenly feels much less empty than advertised.

choices.label: Run for the fallow fields before the hunt gathers.
Go to ground in the gorse and let it pass over.

nodes.body: The counted steps hold, every single one, and the moor lets you across like a toll keeper paid in full. By last light the tussocks give way to fallow fields, gold and tangled. The day's tension drains out through your boot soles. Ahead, over a fold in the land that the fields keep hidden, a slow funnel of crows turns against the failing sky, patient as a drawn circle, marking the place you have walked toward all day.

choices.label: Make for the crows.
Read the last waystone properly before the fields.
Cut the flooded ford to save a mile.

nodes.body: You trust your own eye for one step, and the moor corrects you. Your boot finds not tussock but glass still water, and the pool swallows you to the armpits, cold as charity, sucking with a patience that feels personal. Rope and four sets of hands haul against it while Elowen calls the count. You finish the crossing shivering on her steps, exactly as instructed, and nobody says anything, which is somehow worse than if they had.

choices.label: Wring out and make for the crow fields.
Thrash for the far bank alone.

nodes.body: The pool is deeper than any moor pool has a right to be, and the cold takes the strength from your arms faster than the rope can possibly reach you. The last thing is Elowen's voice, still counting the safe steps, as if the right number could call you back to the path. The moor keeps what it catches, and the drovers always knew, which is why the track goes around.

The second snare takes Bram across the shin, sinew and a whipped blackthorn stave, and his blood fills his boot while his language peels lichen off the waystones. Elowen binds it tight and he walks on it, because he is Bram, but the pace is gone. You reach the fallow fields limping under the crow funnel, having paid in red for the hour you tried to save.

choices.label: Bind it and make for the crows.

nodes.body: Gorse needles and held breath. The hunt arrives in trotting files, small spears at the slope, and one warg on a chain casting for scent up and down the track, wet nose working. It comes a spear length from your face, close enough to count teeth, before a rabbit's ghost yanks it sideways and the handler hauls it off cursing. The files trot on north. The moor breathes again at dusk, and so, carefully, do you.

choices.label: Rise and slip to the crow fields.
Crawl the ditch line out early while they cast.

nodes.body: The ditch line runs out into open turf directly under the warg's turning head, and after that it is thrown nets and hot animal breath and the ground hitting your back. They truss you like prize hams and carry you north at a jog, laughing in their flat tongue about the count. The rescue has become the rescued, and whoever comes for the caravan now will have four more names to bring home.

The crow field at last. In a fold of the land the fields keep hidden lies the caravan, four wains overturned in a rough ring, their wheels turned up to the sky like dead beetles. Wool and cider and tin lie strewn and rain spoiled between them. There are no people anywhere, none, and somehow that is worse than the alternative. The crows walk the wreck like landlords inspecting a property, unhurried, and they barely trouble themselves to hop aside as you come down the slope.

choices.label: Search the wreck methodically.
Call out for survivors.
Let Elowen chalk a finding circle first.
Circle the field edge reading the ground before you go in.
Circle the whole field perimeter first.
Search Tallows overturned office wain.

nodes.body: You go through the wreck methodically, hands steady, the way you would clear a field of stones. Wool bales slashed, cider casks staved, the strongbox rack standing empty. On the lead wain board someone has chalked the manifest in a careful merchant's hand, and one entry is underlined twice. Sealed crate for the abbey at Northgate, reliquary of the first seal, freight paid double. By the count, it is the only cargo the raiders actually took, and everything else they simply broke.

choices.label: Search on for any of the people.
Pick the whole field over for gear worth saving.
Knuckle test the lead wains double floor.

nodes.body: You call out over the field, and the crows answer first, storming up in a black sheet that turns the light dirty. Under their racket, faint but stubborn, comes another sound, and it takes a moment to believe it. From beneath the overturned lead wain, something is knocking. Not random, not settling timber. Three taps, a rest, three taps, the patient rhythm of somebody who has been knocking for a long time and refuses to stop.

choices.label: Run to the lead wain.
Read the crows themselves before you run.

nodes.body: Elowen chalks her finding circle on the wain board and blows across it, and the dust rises inside the ring to show what passed. Small running feet by the dozen, swirling in from the ditch line. Boots driven in a line northeast, dragging. And straight through the middle of it all, untroubled and unhurried, one pair of soft shoes, walking as though the whole ambush were a garden. Elowen stares at that last track for a long time before she speaks.

choices.label: Follow the picture to the lead wain.
Sketch the soft shoed walker for later and search on.
Check under the third wain where the dust shows small footprints.

nodes.body: The field edge tells its own tale to anyone patient enough to walk it. Arrows fletched in cheap black feathers lie gathered like dropped quills, all loosed in one volley from the ditch. Rope ends cut in hanks. Drag paths pointing northeast toward the hills, and blood browned on a wheel rim, days old. It was quick, and it was planned, and whoever planned it wanted the people walking, not dead. That is either mercy or arithmetic.

choices.label: Close in on the wreck ring.

nodes.body: There are no bodies anywhere in the ring, which is its own strange mercy. You are still learning how to feel about that when you hear it. From beneath the overturned lead wain comes a knocking, weak but stubborn. Something alive is under the smashed casks and the settled weight of the wain bed. It has heard your boots, and it is spending strength it cannot spare to reach you.

choices.label: Get the wain off whoever knocks.

nodes.body: You pick the whole field over for what serves, rope and lamp oil and a sound wheel spoke, and you take too long about it. The crows go up all at once, every bird in the field, for a reason that walks on two legs. Goblin stragglers come loping back over the far hedge, a dozen of them, returning to pick the picked bones. The wreck ring stops being a salvage yard. It becomes a trap, and you are standing in it.

choices.label: Scatter into the ditch line and regroup at the lead wain.
Stand in the wreck ring and fight.

nodes.body: Ditch mud and held breath while small feet patter the field above your heads. One bark of goblin laughter rings out over the wreck, something kicked over for the joy of it, and then the sounds trail away north and the field remembers how to be quiet. You regroup at the lead wain scraped and filthy. Under it, patient as ever, the knocking has not stopped.

choices.label: Get the wain off whoever knocks.

nodes.body: The wreck ring makes a poor fort, and the stragglers know their business better than you know yours. Hooks and nets come from every side at once, and numbers finish what surprise started. They truss the four of you like market geese and march you northeast, laughing, prizes from a field already picked. The rescue has become the rescued, and the caravan's rescuers will need rescuers of their own.

Under the wain bed, in a hollow of smashed casks, a man lies pinned with his leg bent wrong beneath the axle. Gray faced, cracked lips, a scout's oiled cloak twisted around him. He has been three days listening to crows learn new words, he croaks, and his name is Finch, and if you are the rescue then the marshal has a strange sense of humor. His eyes are fever bright, but they count your company and land on your packs, measuring what you have to work with.

choices.label: Lever the wain with a wheel spoke, slow and sure.
Lift it on Brams count, fast and hard.
Dig the casks out from around him first.
Send Elowen for fever herbs before you lift anything.
Fetch the wagon jack from the office wain.

nodes.body: The wheel spoke makes a lever, and the wain creaks up a hand at a time, slow and sure, while Sable slides Finch clear smooth as a knife from a sheath. Elowen splints the leg with spoke wood and herb craft, working fast in the failing light. His grip finds your wrist while she ties off. A scout's luck rides with whoever pulled him out of the dark, he says, and by his face he means it as a contract.

choices.label: Hear everything he knows.

nodes.body: You take the lift on Bram's count, every back at once, and the wain rises a full foot with a groan of abused timber. Finch comes out in one dragged rush. Then Bram's boot slips in the cask wreck, and the weight comes down like a falling roof, catching shoulders and pride together. Bruises for everyone, but the scout is free, fevered and grateful past words, gripping hands blindly while Elowen sets to work.

choices.label: Splint him and hear what he knows.

nodes.body: You dig the casks out from around him first, patient as drystone work, rolling each one clear while the wain settles a little further with every gap you open. Finch whispers at you to hurry, which does not help, and then apologizes, which does not either. At the end one cask remains, pinned under the axle itself, carrying weight that nothing should carry. The next move matters more than all the others put together.

choices.label: Lever the axle now, no more time.
Wedge the axle with the spare wheel first.

nodes.body: You lever the axle on nerve alone, no time left for cleverness, and the wain lurches like a waking animal. Finch comes out snatched by the cloak with the boards kissing his boot heels, alive and white as chalk, and you are no better. For a moment everyone just breathes. Then Elowen crawls to the leg with her satchel and begins, and the ordinary work of splints is the sweetest sound of the day.

choices.label: Splint him and hear what he knows.

nodes.body: The spare wheel wedges the axle true, and after that the lift is almost easy, the wain giving Finch up without a fight. He comes out clean and props against a cask hoop, managing half a grin under the fever. Driest rescue he has had all week, he says, and asks whether the marshal sent you or whether you are just the sort that follows crows. Either way, he adds, his leg and his gratitude are yours.

choices.label: Splint him and hear what he knows.

nodes.body: Finch tells it all by shaded lamplight, reporting like the scout he is. Goblins by the score at dusk, arrows from the ditch, everyone living roped and marched northeast at spear point, and a man in ash gray robes who paid the chief in old silver and counted the crate like a father counting a child. Then his map scrap comes out. Causeway, goat path, culvert, charcoal tunnels, four ways into the hills, and the Redtooth horn calls besides, two short for all clear, long and falling for hunt loosed. Outside, night is coming on with a storm behind it, and the storm will choose your camp if you do not.

choices.label: Fire in the deep hollow.
Cold camp in the hedge bones.
Push the trail through the storm dark.
Rig Finch a shelter first, whatever it costs.
Shelter in the old field barrow with the leaning stone.
Take first watch beside Bram.

nodes.body: You build the fire down in the deep hollow where the light cannot carry, and it earns its keep. Wet boots steam, stew comes together out of rations, and warmth works into bones that had forgotten owning any. The company eats like a threshing crew and sleeps in shifts. Past midnight, on your watch, eyes appear at the edge of the firelight. Low, paired, green, and patient, more of them each time the flames snap, watching the warmth the way you watched the notice board.

choices.label: Wake everyone slow and stand to arms.
Throw light, shout, and rush the eyes.
Sit Sables knife lesson by the settling embers.

nodes.body: You wake the others slow, a hand to each shoulder, and the company stands to arms without one voice raised. Iron and torches make a quiet wall, and the eyes at the firelight edge do their counting. Wolves after all, not the other thing, and wolves can count better than most tax men. The pack melts north before the storm breaks, and you sleep in watches to dawn with the rain drumming the hollow's lip above you.

choices.label: Sleep in watches to dawn.

nodes.body: You throw light, shout, and rush the eyes, which turn out to belong to wolves with strong opinions about being rushed. The scramble in the wet dark is all snap and scuffle and somebody's elbow, and it ends with one gray body cooling in the bracken and one bitten forearm, yours. Elowen binds it by firelight with her mouth set in a line that does the scolding for her. You sleep in watches, throbbing.

choices.label: Bind it and sleep in watches.

nodes.body: You make a cold camp in the hedge bones, and the storm arrives on schedule to punish the choice. Rain comes down like thrown gravel for half the night. You sleep in shivers and short watches, curled against packs, and no light burns to call anything down on you, which was the whole bargain. By the small hours the storm drags its skirts away east, and the last watch sees stars, cold and rinsed and very far away.

choices.label: Rise stiff at dawn.
Read the hedge bones properly by lantern shade.

nodes.body: You push the trail through the storm, and the storm pushes back. Lightning prints the fields white and gone, white and gone, and somewhere in the third mile a washed out bank gives way under your boots and drops you into a ditch running like a millrace. The night is charging a toll for every mile, and it does not take promises. Ahead the drag trail is drowning, and behind, the wreck has already vanished into the rain.

choices.label: Haul out and shelter till the worst passes.
Keep pushing hard through the flood dark.

nodes.body: The second washout is worse than the first, and it takes your ankle sideways in a drowned rut with a wet crack you feel in your teeth. Fever arrives by morning, riding the cold. The company carries you to a drover's hut and Elowen fights the fever for three days, and wins, for whatever winning is worth by then. The trail is dead cold before you can stand on the ankle, and the season closes without you.

You rig Finch a shelter first, whatever the hour costs, wain boards and oiled cloak angled against the coming rain, water and waybread in easy reach of his good arm. His voice follows you out into the wind. Send word to Hedda by the first honest traveler, he calls, and mind the horns. The storm night passes in the lee of the wreck itself, loud and long, and the shelter holds like a promise kept.

choices.label: Rise at dawn and take the drag trail.
Spend the shelter hour flat over Finchs map arguing ways in.

nodes.body: Dawn on the drag trail. Rope fibers on thorns, and a child's shoe set neatly on a stone, as if someone wanted it found and grieved over properly. By midday the trail drops into a sunken lane, and there sits a stuck cart with a willow cage lashed to its bed, one lamed goblin driver cursing a mule in two languages. In the cage, watching you through the withies with his chin up, sits a boy in drover browns.

choices.label: Rush the cart before it matters.
Let Sable parley from the bank.
Shadow the cart and take it after dark.
Set Sables tin whistle decoy beyond the hedges.
Rush the sunken lane before reading it.

nodes.body: You rush the cart, and the rush covers half the lane before the driver even gets its horn to its lips. Bram's axe haft ends the note early, one short clack of wood on horn, and the goblin folds sideways into the ditch. The mule bolts on principle, and the cage cart slews around into the bank with the boy inside shouting encouragement to absolutely everyone. The lane goes quiet except for the mule's opinion fading east.

choices.label: Cut the boy free.
Chase the bolting mule down the lane.

nodes.body: You chase the bolting mule around the lane's blind bend, and the bend has other plans. The returning escort is filing through it, spears at the slope, and the sunken lane holds you like its own cage, banks too steep and nowhere to be. Spears come from three sides. The last thing you see clearly is a red tooth grinning on a bark shield, close up, and then the lane keeps what it caught.

Sable's lazy voice drifts down from the bank in trade pidgin, offering one full ration sack for one skinny boy, take it or keep him. The driver weighs its spear against the sack, with the mule dead lame and the escort a mile gone ahead. Commerce wins, the way commerce has been winning since before kingdoms had names. The trade is made ugly but made. The driver hobbles its mule away without one backward look.

choices.label: Unlash the cage and see to the boy.
Walk a while beside the boy and let him talk.

nodes.body: You shadow the cart to dusk through the hedgerows, patience like a toothache, waiting while it lurches and sticks and lurches onward again. At dark the driver camps in a fold of the lane, loops the cage rope around its own wrist, and sleeps with its spear across its knees while the fire burns down to embers. The boy in the cage does not sleep. Knives or nerve, then, and the night is exactly long enough for either.

choices.label: Cut the rope and lift the boy out quiet.
Take the whole cart, mule and all.
Go through the drivers satchel on the lashing.

nodes.body: The mule has opinions about strangers at midnight, and shares them at war horn volume. The driver wakes swinging, the cage lashings tangle, and the whole tidy plan becomes a scramble in the dark, boy out, packs up, run. Behind you the braying gathers answers from the east, none of them friendly. You run the hedges until the noise gives up, lungs burning, the boy matching you stride for stride.

choices.label: Run east with the boy and lose the noise.

nodes.body: Out of the willow cage comes Tam of Dunmere, thirteen and furious and trying very hard not to cry, and managing it, mostly. Between bites of waybread he gives up everything he hoarded. The walkers were driven to the stone keep on the hill. The goblins call their chief Vessk. The ash robed one they all step wide of is named Malverin, and a guard bragged that the stone door under the keep opens at moonset three days hence, when the seal thing sings. Then you point him south with waybread and a message for Hedda learned by heart, and he walks backward waving until the hedge takes him.

choices.label: Turn northeast for the Hollow Hills.

nodes.body: You reach the Hollow Hills at dusk and climb a heather spur to look at the problem properly. Gray whalebacks rise one behind another into the haze, and on the second ridge squats Harrowstone, broken crowned, its watch fires already lit like sullen stars. Finch's map lives in your memory now, and it offers four ways in. The causeway and its gate, bold as a knock. The goat path up the north cliff. The old culvert under the east wall, and the charcoal burners' tunnels that go into the hill itself. Behind the wind, faint and steady, the drums keep their time.

choices.label: Walk up the causeway bold as brass.
Climb the goat path up the north face.
Find the culvert mouth under the east wall.
Enter the charcoal burners tunnels.

nodes.body: The causeway runs at the keep like a stone tongue laid over black bog, and the wind snaps the torch flames on the gatehouse ahead. Halfway along, somebody has set up a toll of sorts. Two goblin spears lean against a glowing brazier while their owners crouch over a dice game, playing for teeth by the sound of the arguing. Beyond them the arch waits, and under everything the bog breathes its cold tar breath, patient on both sides of the stone.

choices.label: March up like you are expected and paid for.
Crawl the causeway shadow line under the parapet.
Wade the bog margin and flank the brazier.
Swing up the drain chain to the gatehouse roof.
Wade the arch shadows under the causeway itself.
Wait for the change horn that empties the brazier post.

nodes.body: You walk up chin high, stride purposeful, muttering just loud enough about silver owed to the ash robed one, and the phrase lands like a password half remembered. The dicers squint into the dark at you. One rises with its head tilted over, smelling wrongness the way a dog smells weather, but slow about naming it, because nobody marches up the causeway who is not supposed to be there. The brazier crackles. The moment balances on a knife's width.

choices.label: Toss them a coin and keep walking.
Bark at them in your best angry trader voice.
Keep silent and keep walking when they hail you.

nodes.body: You take the shadow line under the parapet on elbows and knees, cold stone printing itself into your forearms, while the dice game rattles along a spear length above your head. It is going well, as crawls go, right up until a thrown die bounces off the parapet lip, drops, and rolls to a stop against your knuckle. Above you, a goblin voice complains about the throw and a stool scrapes. Somebody is coming to fetch it.

choices.label: Freeze and let them fetch it.
Palm the die into the bog and flow on.
Rise and dash the last stretch to the arch.
Check the chain locker grating in the footing.

nodes.body: You slip off the stone into the bog margin and wade the flank, water to your waist and every step a negotiation with the sucking cold. The brazier light wobbles on the black water as you edge wide of the toll post. Then something long and smooth slides past your shin, unhurried, the way large things are unhurried, and continues on into the dark with the water barely moving over it. Every instinct you own files an objection.

choices.label: Hold still until it passes.
Push hard for the gate footing now.
Study the pale lights drifting over the bog around you.

nodes.body: You toss them a coin without breaking stride, and the coin spins firelit into a leather palm that snaps shut like a trap. The toll of the world works on goblins too. The dicer grunts, entirely satisfied by the arithmetic, and waves you past into the shadow of the gatehouse arch, already turning back to its game and its argument about teeth. The wicket gate stands ajar beyond, and you take it.

choices.label: Slip through the wicket gate into the outer ward.

nodes.body: You bark at them in your best angry trader voice, all wagons and schedules and whose fault the delay is, and it lands half right, which turns out to be enough. The dicers begin arguing with each other about whose fault you are, each certain the other was told. The argument becomes your door. You stride under the arch unquestioned while the blame ricochets behind you, and the outer ward opens ahead in firelight and shadow.

choices.label: Cross into the outer ward.

nodes.body: The older dicer finally names the wrongness it has been smelling, and its horn comes up faster than your excuse. The causeway fills from both ends with spears at a jog, and there is no bog deep enough to hide in and no dark thick enough to run through. They take you to the cells with the toll coin still in your pocket, which one of them collects on the way, for the game.

You freeze, hood down, one more shadow among shadows, while the fetching hand gropes along the stone a hand's width from your face. Fingers close on the die. They withdraw with their prize, and the game resumes above you as if the world had not just balanced on a knucklebone. Your heartbeat tries twice to give you away anyway. The long crawl finishes under the arch, and you breathe again in the wicket shadow.

choices.label: Slip into the outer ward.

nodes.body: You palm the die into the bog with a flick, and the splash is smaller than the consequence. The game dissolves into accusations of theft, goblin voices climbing over each other, both spears forgotten against the brazier while the players turn out each other's pouches. The causeway shadow carries you the rest of the way to the arch at a walk. Behind you the argument is just getting properly started, and it sounds like it has years left in it.

choices.label: Slip into the outer ward.

nodes.body: You hold absolutely still with the water at your chest. The smooth thing coils past once, then twice, testing your shape against its memory of drowned logs. Then it loses interest, the way patient things do. The bog lets you go with a sound like a kiss. You reach the gate footing mud blind and shivering, fingers barely answering, and start up the wet masonry toward the wicket gate.

choices.label: Climb the footing to the wicket gate.

nodes.body: You push hard for the footing and the push wakes the bog, mud climbing to your chest with an appetite. The long smooth thing turns back with what can only be called purpose. Bram's arm finds your collar while Sable throws knives at ripples, swearing, and the argument between his grip and the bog's is brief and loud. You come free like a cork, minus a boot's worth of dignity, and scramble for the stone.

choices.label: Kick free and scramble to the gate footing.
Tear loose whatever it costs.

nodes.body: The bog wins the argument. Black water closes over the torchlight, and the cold takes the fight out of you with terrifying efficiency. They haul you out at last on the wrong side of empty lungs and pump the water from you on the causeway stones. You wake wrapped in cloaks, being carried west, and the hills are done with you this season, and you with them.

A horn sounds from the gatehouse while the arch is still fifty paces off, long and falling, the hunt loosed call, and the night answers it from three directions. There is nothing left but the running retreat back down the causeway, spears rattling behind you and the bog laughing in the dark on both sides. The hills stay shut, and moonset does not wait on second attempts.

The north face stands silver and black by moonlight, and the goat path is a rumor of ledges stitched up it, more suggestion than road. Wind combs the heather flat around your boots. Sable is already ten feet up the first pitch, hair whipping, calling down that it is easier than it looks. She is lying, and everyone knows she is lying, and somehow the lie helps anyway. Far above, the broken tower cuts a notch out of the stars.

choices.label: Climb steady, testing every hold.
Climb fast between the wind gusts.
Rope the party together first.
Let Sable hunt the cliff for the old warden stair.
Wait out the deep watch in a cliff hollow first.
Deal with the hawks eyrie blocking the second pitch.

nodes.body: You climb steady, testing every hold before it takes weight. The valley tilts away beneath your heels a farm at a time. A ledge at the halfway mark offers room to stand and breathe and not look down, and everyone uses it in different proportions. Above it the path pinches to nothing around a bulge of rock, smooth as a kettle and twice as unfriendly. The wind is picking up an opinion about whoever goes first.

choices.label: Lead around the bulge yourself.
Let Sable find the line and follow her calls.
Read the rising wind and call the climb off.
Climb to the old beacon bracket for the sight line.

nodes.body: You climb fast between the gusts, fingers jammed in cracks while the wind tries its luck against your back. It is good time made and the blood sings with it, the halfway ledge arriving almost before the fear does. Above waits the bulge, a swell of smooth rock around which the path simply stops existing for eight feet, holding its breath like everything else on this cliff.

choices.label: Keep the pace around the bulge.
Steady down and follow Sables line.
Haul the pack strap instead of the next hold.

nodes.body: You rope the party together first, waist to waist, and the climbing goes slower for the tangling but honest all the way. The halfway ledge collects you one by one, breathing hard. Elowen arrives white knuckled and stands with her back to the stone, counting her breaths in fours like a good soldier, and nobody remarks on it, because Bram is doing the same thing in sixes. The bulge waits overhead, smooth as a lie.

choices.label: Send Sable ahead on the rope around the bulge.
Take the lead yourself with the rope paid out behind.
Wait out the wind on the ledge, roped and patient.

nodes.body: You lead around the bulge yourself, on friction and nerve and nothing else. One foot swims in open air. Two fingers keep the argument going in a crack that goblin masons cut before your grandmother was born. For three heartbeats the mountain owns you entirely. Then the path opens again beyond, wide as a cart track by comparison, and runs up toward the wall base like a stroll it was saving for you.

choices.label: Walk the last ledges to the broken wall.

nodes.body: Sable finds the line and her calls float down the rock, left hand high, weight on the toe, trust the black knob, it is kinder than it looks. Her voice threads you through holds you would never have seen in daylight, let alone dark. The bulge passes underneath you almost before the fear catches up, and then the whole party is across and grinning at each other like fools on the far side.

choices.label: Walk the last ledges to the broken wall.

nodes.body: You keep the pace, and the wind cheats. A gust hits the bulge like a shoulder charge exactly as your boots commit, and suddenly you are skating on smooth stone with the valley underneath your heels and the mountain deciding at its leisure whether to keep you. The crack takes your fingers to the second knuckle. The gust leans harder, patient, testing the grip against the fall.

choices.label: Jam in and wait out the gust.
Lunge for the next hold through it.

nodes.body: The lunge finds air where rock should be. The cliff turns over once, twice, with a terrible slowness, and the heather comes up like a fist. The Hollow Hills keep their oldest toll, the one they were charging before goblins, before wardens, before anyone at all thought to build a keep on top. The wind combs the heather flat again, and the night goes on without you.

The wall base arrives where the cliff has done the masons' work for them. A breach of tumbled stone spills from under the broken tower, climbable in a dozen places. Above it runs a sentry walk, and along it goes one bored goblin, dragging a spear butt against the parapet the way a boy drags a stick along a fence. Its circuit is long. Its attention is elsewhere. That is an invitation, with conditions attached.

choices.label: Time the sentrys circuit and slip the breach.
Send Sable up to quiet the sentry first.
Wait for cloud over the moon, then all at once.

nodes.body: You count the sentry's circuit three times to be sure, and it comes out the same each time. Forty heartbeats of empty parapet between passes, regular as a water clock. On the fourth count the company takes the breach single file, hands and knees on the tumbled stone, while the spear butt scrapes away into the dark above. You drop into the outer ward with eleven heartbeats to spare.

choices.label: Drop into the outer ward.

nodes.body: Sable goes up the tumbled stone like a thought going up a spine. The scuffle above lasts no longer than a roost settling. Then her face appears over the parapet wearing the sentry's iron cap at an angle that should be illegal, and she beckons with two fingers. The company climbs through the breach and drops into the outer ward one by one, under the watchful authority of a borrowed hat.

choices.label: Climb through and drop into the ward.

nodes.body: You wait for cloud, and the moon obliges on its own unhurried schedule. The rush goes up the tumbled stones in a clatter that the wind mostly covers. Mostly. Somewhere in the dark above and behind, a goblin voice calls a question, once, in the tone of somebody who does not really want an answer because an answer would mean work. The company freezes on the stones, half in and half out of the breach.

choices.label: Freeze against the wall until it loses interest.
Drop into the ward fast and scatter to shadow.

nodes.body: The question comes twice and gets shrugged away, the wind blamed as sentries have blamed the wind since walls were invented. You cross the last of the breach at a crawl, cheek against stone that holds a century of frost. You come down into the outer ward's shadow with your heart hammering a rhythm the deep drums would envy. The keep accepts one more secret into its collection.

choices.label: Drop into the outer ward.

nodes.body: The wind rises to a decision at the halfway ledge, and the decision is no. No way up past the bulge in this, and the way down has become a slide into darkness. Dawn finds the party still pinned to the ledge like moths on a board, and the goblin ropes come down from the sentry walk almost politely. Prisoners taken by rope and patience, without one spear leveled, which stings in its own way.

The mountain says no in gust after gust until even Sable calls it, which is how you know it is truly called. The climb surrenders a ledge at a time, down and down, until the heather takes your boots again. Behind you the keep stands unentered with its watch fires burning, and only moonset left to matter, and moonset does not wait for the wind to change its mind.

The culvert mouth opens in the east ditch, breathing cold like a cellar door. Its rusted grate hangs by one hinge, and knee deep water slides out of a dark so complete it feels solid. Somewhere far up the pipe a slow drip keeps time, patient as a clock in an empty house. This is the surveyor's promise, drains east under the wall, and it will keep that promise to anyone who can cross its dark to collect.

choices.label: Light the brass lantern and shutter it low.
Feel your way in through the dark.
Send Sable ahead striking tinder sparks.
Sound the pipe mouths by knuckle tap first.
Dam the flow at the mouth before you wade.
Start Elowens counting song and walk it in.

nodes.body: The shuttered beam turns the drain honest. Lantern light finds a dry ledge running above the waterline, and side mouths marked with goblin tallow smears, somebody's private wayfinding. Wedged behind a fallen block sits a waxed satchel. Inside it, oil and dried eel, some goblin's secret stash against hungry watches. The drain keeps its secrets poorly against a brass lantern. That is exactly why you carried it three days uphill.

choices.label: Walk the dry ledge to the inner grate.
Check the tallow marked side mouth first.
Search the side chamber off the dry ledge.

nodes.body: The dark comes down like a hand over your face, and you go in under it. One palm on the right wall, always the right, while the water climbs thigh high and patient. Side passages breathe on your cheek as they pass, each a small cold question, and the count of your steps becomes the only map you own. Behind you the company wades in your wake, linked hand to shoulder, trusting your arithmetic.

choices.label: Keep the wall and count on.
Wade the faster center channel.
Shout once up the pipe to test the echo.
Take the third side mouth on a guess.

nodes.body: Sable strikes tinder sparks ahead, slow lightning that makes the drain jump into being and vanish again. She calls the shapes between flashes, ledge left, block right, low beam, her voice flat and sure. It works, mostly. But somewhere in the flash and dark the counting of side mouths goes wrong, five where there should be four, and an honest doubt starts wading along behind the party, keeping pace.

choices.label: Trust her count to the inner grate.
Stop and recount at the next spark.

nodes.body: The inner grate chamber opens ahead, and the lantern shows a lock pin rusted down to iron lace. Oil and patience ease it out whole, and rags muffle the hinges as the grate swings. Beyond, worn stairs climb toward a thin line of firelight, the outer ward's underside. You shutter the lantern and take the steps one at a time, wet boots on old stone, into the keep at last.

choices.label: Climb into the ward.

nodes.body: The tallow marked mouth opens on a goblin bolt hole, snug as a den. Dry bracken bedding, a tally wall of scratched teeth counting somebody's private ledger, and on a rough shelf, a watch horn on a strap. The horn is worth more than any eel, and it comes away quiet as a thought. Whoever nests here will come home poorer tonight, and the keep's own voice now hangs at your belt.

choices.label: Back out and take the ledge to the inner grate.

nodes.body: The wall count holds true, and your fingers find the inner grate in the blind dark. The lock pin works loose by feel, skinned knuckles and whispered arithmetic sharing the labor between them. The hinge gives one dreadful creak, loud as a shout in the pipe, before wet rags strangle it quiet. You wait through ten heartbeats of listening dark, and nothing comes. The grate stands open, and the stairs breathe ward air down at you.

choices.label: Ease it open and climb into the ward.
Force it in one fast shove and rush the stairs.

nodes.body: The center channel runs deeper than reason, chest cold and quick, but faster than the ledges by half. Then something long bumps your leg, once and then twice, curious in the way eels are curious about drowned things that still move. It is not hurrying, because nothing down here hurries except hearts. The wall waits two strides away on your right, and the bump comes a third time, noticeably more interested.

choices.label: Back slow to the wall and go on by touch.
Stab at the bump and push through.

nodes.body: The recount finds the error, one side mouth taken for the main run three verses back. You stand in a cistern chamber under a chimney of rungs rotted soft as bread, with black water at your knees and your own breathing loud in the round stone dark. The draft tells the truth of the way back, cold on your wet cheek, steady as a pointing finger. The rungs tell a different story, and it is shorter.

choices.label: Follow the draft back to the main run.
Risk the rotten rungs straight up.

nodes.body: The grate screech runs up the stones like a struck bell, and the keep answers it. Boots and torches converge on the stair head while you are still blinking at your own hands, and the drain refills behind you with spears and lamplight. They take you wet and shivering at the foot of the stairs, and the cells are at least dry, which is the only kind thing anyone says about them.

You stab at the bump, and the answer comes back in kind from something with far more practice. The channel pulls the light out of the world in one long cold swallow. The drain keeps its oldest secret, one keeper richer, and the slow drip keeps time over the black water the way it has for two hundred years, unhurried, missing nothing.

The third rung goes to wet powder under your boot. The fall is short but the clatter is long, ringing up the chimney and out into the night above. You grope the long way back to the ditch by dead reckoning and luck, and surface to find the culvert mouth watched now, torchlight pacing the wall above it. Moonset is one night nearer, and you have nothing to show but wet boots.

The burners' dell opens on the hill's flank, old kilns standing around it like beehives gone cold. Tunnel mouths gape between them, propped with charred timber, going into the hill itself. One kiln still lives, banked and breathing, and beside it sits a soot black old woman who watches your company arrive the way a shopkeeper watches a delivery she ordered weeks ago. You have the strange feeling of being expected, and of being late.

choices.label: Ask the old burner for the way through.
Take the widest cart tunnel.
Take the narrow tunnel with the breathing draft.
Cross kiln to kiln and study the cart tunnel first.
Hear out the singed young burner selling a quick way.
Ask Mag about the wardens and her grandmothers day.

nodes.body: Mag the burner trades her knowledge for town news and a long look at each of your faces, and calls it a fair price, which it is. Her tarred string goes into your hand, one end already tied off somewhere in the dark. Follow it to the chimney, she says, and mind the third fork. The sweet smelling way is the dying way. Her kiln warmth soaks into your cold bones while she talks, and you leave the dell steadier than you entered it.

choices.label: Follow the tarred string in.
Ask Mag to open the kiln heart itself.

nodes.body: The cart tunnel runs wide and rutted into the hill, fresh spoil heaped along its walls. Then light swings ahead, and you flatten into the dark. A goblin dig crew comes trudging out with barrows, and their cargo rattles dry and pale in the lamplight. Bones. Crypt spoil, hauled out of the deep levels like potatoes, barrow after barrow of somebody's ancestors on their way to the boneworks.

choices.label: Hide in a side pocket and let them pass.
Back out quiet and take the draft tunnel.
Bluff the dig crew in trade pidgin.
Follow the diggings deeper toward the old air.
Learn the dig crews shift song through the props.

nodes.body: The draft tunnel closes around you tight as a sleeve, and the old props groan their learned complaints as you pass. Charcoal grit settles in your teeth. But the draft keeps moving on your face, cool and steady, promising that this tunnel goes somewhere the hill breathes. Halfway along, one prop leans worse than its brothers, cracked through and holding out of habit, and the ceiling above it has begun, very quietly, to sag.

choices.label: Shore the worst prop with a spare timber and go steady.
Squeeze through fast and trust the props.
Follow the sweeter smelling side mouth where the air runs warm.

nodes.body: The string runs true through three forks, and at the third the sweet smelling mouth breathes at you exactly as promised, warm and drowsy, the air itself like a lullaby. You hold your breath past it. The string ends at a knot around a stone under a soot chimney, and the handholds cut into its throat are old and sure, warden work. Far above, a circle of gray night sky waits like the top of a well.

choices.label: Climb the chimney into the ward.

nodes.body: The dig crew trudges past a boot length away, complaining in goblin about quotas and the ash robed one's temper. They pass close enough to count the teeth strung on their belts. One barrow stays behind, ticking over on a broken wheel. Then the tunnel stands empty. The way ahead runs open to the spoil chute, with the crew's lamplight dwindling toward the dell and the hill's cold dark waiting ahead.

choices.label: Slip up the spoil chute into the ward.
Pilfer the barrow first for anything telling.
Hold the pocket while a second crew passes too.
Pull the manifest board off the broken barrow.

nodes.body: The barrow yields one coin among the bones, and the coin is worth the stop. Silver, old as kings, stamped with a crown that no living herald would know. The ash robed one's pay dirt, sitting in your palm with two centuries of dark on it. Then footsteps sound, returning up the tunnel, and the lesson about curiosity and timing arrives at a trot with lamplight behind it.

choices.label: Pocket it and take the spoil chute now.

nodes.body: You shore the worst prop with a spare timber, patient work in bad light, and the sleeve of rock lets you pass at a steady crawl. The tunnel opens at last onto a junction under a spoil chute, cart ruts crossing below it, the hill above breathing slow and cold through the gap. Behind you the shored prop holds, and will hold for the next fool too, which is more than it promised.

choices.label: Climb the spoil chute into the ward.

nodes.body: You squeeze through fast, and your shoulder catches the cracked prop square. The ceiling clears its throat. What follows is a scramble through raining grit with the hill grinding shut behind you like a slow door, dust rolling past your ears in the dark. You come out at the junction under the spoil chute bruised and coughing, one boot's width ahead of the roof, which settles behind you with terrible satisfaction.

choices.label: Climb the spoil chute bruised but through.
Go back for the dropped pack under the groan.

nodes.body: The ceiling keeps its promise. It takes four pairs of hands the rest of the night to dig you out, and the tunnel is gone behind you when they do, roof and props and all. You are alive and own one boot between two feet, which the company counts as arithmetic in your favor. The hill has closed itself to you, and moonset closes in behind it.

The sweet smelling way grows sweeter with every step, warm as a kitchen and kind as sleep, until the lantern of your mind gutters without your noticing. You wake outside in the dell, dragged by soot black hands, with Mag shaking her head over you like a disappointed aunt. One whole night gone, she says, and your strength with it. The bad air keeps its own toll gate, and you paid in hours.

The side pocket is good, but the one goblin with a nose is better. Barrows drop, picks come up, and the dig crew proves that diggers can also count, loudly, in a ring around your hiding place. The count is four, and the destination is the cells. You ride there in the barrows, among the bones, which is the sort of joke goblins find funny the whole way.

The outer ward of Harrowstone spreads before you in firelight and shadow.
