And the east shows gray as the company walks out the broken gate, unopposed, into the ordinary miracle of morning.

choices.label: Take the road home to Barrowmere.
Parade the bound wizard once around the ward first.

nodes.body: In the quiet after the door, with Redtooth drums sounding retreat far above, Vessk himself waits at the antechamber arch with his honor guard. He looks a long time at the shut door, and at the box in your arms, and says his grandmother was right about everything. The bargain is done. The Redtooth march at dawn, and the prisoners walk free, his word on it, which tonight is worth more than any coin in the hill.

choices.label: March everyone home under a goblin chiefs strange safe conduct.

nodes.body: The ditch gives onto drover country, and nine freed souls and four spent ones move hedge to hedge while the keep burns torchlight at its own shadows behind you. Tallow leads by ways only carters know, culvert and cattle track and dry ford. The box rides wrapped in a cloak on your back, still humming its one low note into your spine. And the last miles walk themselves into a dawn the color of pewter.

choices.label: Walk the last miles into dawn.

nodes.body: The warden's ways give out at last through a fox thicket door in a dell two hills south of everything. Stars hang overhead like spilled salt. The long way round begins on aching legs, and the box goes quiet at last against your back, as if it knows it is walking away from the door it sings to. Nobody talks for the first mile, and there is nothing in any of you but breath and luck.

choices.label: Take the long way home.

nodes.body: Barrowmere at midmorning, and the company walks up the King's Road with nine souls behind them and the reliquary of the first seal in an iron banded box. Hedda meets you at the gate with the whole town at her back, and Finch is there on a crutch, grinning like a split melon. Fifty crowns are counted out on the marshal's own table, and the Company of the Brass Lantern is toasted under its own sign until the candles drown. In the quiet after, Hedda spreads Malverin's burnt notes on the table. One line survived the fire, and she reads it twice before she says it aloud. The second sleeps beneath the Greymarsh.

It is the strangest homecoming Barrowmere has ever seen. A goblin honor guard halts at the third milestone and watches nine freed prisoners and four ragged heroes walk the last stretch alone. Vessk's parting gift is a red tooth drawn on your map over the Greymarsh, with a warning growled in trade tongue. The pale ones dig there too. Hedda pays fifty crowns to a company that made a goblin chief keep his word, and the Brass Lantern hangs its first trophy, a chief's arrow, over the taproom door.

Dawn comes up over the drover country, and Barrowmere rings its bells at the sight of the column. Nine souls are delivered footsore and laughing at nothing, the way the rescued laugh. The reliquary sits on Hedda's table, still faintly singing, while Tallow signs his freight bond closed with a shaking hand. And the marshal reads the one legible line of the wizard's burnt notes twice before she says it aloud to the room. The second sleeps beneath the Greymarsh, and the room goes very quiet around the fifty crowns.

The long way round ends three days late, through the south fords, and the town is half into mourning bands when the company walks in whole. The bells find their voices one by one. The reliquary is delivered with fox thicket burrs still in everyone's hair, and Hedda docks nothing from the fifty crowns. She adds a map besides, rolled and sealed, because the wizard's trail runs south into the Greymarsh, and someone with a brass lantern is going to have to follow it.

In the gate square the watch sergeant takes names by lamplight at a folding table. Along the wall stands a line of Dunmere families holding candles, quiet as fence posts in the cold. It is a vigil for the caravan, and you can tell it has stood every night for two weeks, because nobody in it hopes loudly anymore. The candles simply burn, small and stubborn, against the whole weight of the coming dark, and a wall like that asks questions without saying anything at all.

choices.label: Speak with the families at the vigil.
Press the sergeant for what the watch really knows.
Sign the watch roster for steady wages instead.

nodes.body: A grandmother presses a candle stub into your hand before you can speak, so that you will know them, she says, and then recites the names the way careful people count coins. Tallow the merchant, and Berta his granddaughter, eleven years old and brave past sense, and young Tam who never stops asking questions. Nine souls, with a hundred small facts hung upon them, who limps and who sings and who fears deep water. You leave carrying all of it, and the candle stub in your pocket weighs more than your sword.

choices.label: Carry the names into the inn.

nodes.body: The sergeant answers slowly, in the manner of honest men whose orders embarrass them. The watch is forbidden past the toll shrine by standing orders from somewhere above the marshal's head. Two scouts went anyway on their own boots, and one never came back at all. The other returned raving about red teeth on the waystones and has not slept a whole night since. So Hedda posts coin, he says, because her hands are tied and her conscience is not. He squares his papers and wishes you careful going.

choices.label: Take that into the inn.

nodes.body: You sign the watch roster for steady wages, a warm gatehouse, and a wall to walk. Everyone tells you it is the sensible choice, and everyone is perfectly right. All winter you pace the parapet and watch the east road, and every morning it stretches away empty. Somebody else will have to bring the caravan home now. From up here, at least, you will be the first to see whether anybody ever does.

Josser's oddments crate is a museum of other people's luck, a whetstone, a tin whistle, a mismatched stirrup. Under them lies a water stained page from some old survey, showing the Hollow Hills with a keep drawn on the second ridge, and along its east wall in spidery writing, the words drains east under. Josser wants three coppers for it as waste paper. While you consider, light fingers brush your belt, and a shadow peels away from the stall into the alley with your coin purse.

choices.label: Buy the survey page and head for the inn.
Chase the cutpurse who just lifted your coin.

nodes.body: You chase the cutpurse into his own alleys, which is precisely the problem. He is faster in the dark, and his friends are slower but much larger. You limp out the far end without your coin, carrying bruised ribs and a lasting lesson about chasing shadows into shadows. By the time you breathe properly again, the commission is signed and sealed and walking east under somebody else's boots.

The drover table drinks in silence for the missing, older road men with weather in their faces. One of them tips a little cider onto the boards and draws the drover track in it with a forefinger, marking where the waystones turn wrong past the moor crossing. Count the teeth painted on each stone, he mutters, because the count climbs as you go east, and a count that climbs is counting toward something. Nobody at the table disagrees with him, which is its own kind of proof.

choices.label: Commit the cider map to memory and see the clerk.
Join their talk of a vengeance ride tonight.

nodes.body: The vengeance ride musters at moonrise, half drunk, brave, and utterly shapeless. Warg howls scatter it before the second milestone, and by gray morning the survivors are limping home in twos. Marshal Hedda confiscates every boot involved, yours included, for a fortnight of cooling heels. The commission goes quietly to cooler heads, and the drover table drinks in silence for one name more.

The fiddler is sawing out the old lay of Harrowstone, and half the room hums along without noticing it does so. You know the verses yourself. A king's hall raised on an older hill, a winter that came up from underneath it, and the warden order who sang the hill to sleep and sealed their song behind a stone door. Everyone knows the words, and nobody believes them. Mid verse the fiddler catches your eye and tips her head toward the commission bill, as if to say, funny old songs. You drop a copper in her hat.

choices.label: Drop a copper in her hat and see the clerk.

nodes.body: You set a fresh mug at the dwarf's elbow without a word and settle in to wait him out. He drinks it the same way, wordless, working the awl through the strap while the fire burns down a knuckle. At the bottom of the mug he finally speaks without looking up, in a voice like a door being tested, asking what you will do up there when it turns bad and running looks wise. The whole evening narrows down to your answer.

choices.label: Answer honest, afraid and going anyway.
Say whatever sounds bravest.

nodes.body: You lay your grandmother's short sword on the felt as a stake, and the whole room inhales together. Sable reads the blade a long moment, then reads you considerably longer. She rolls, and wins, and slides the sword straight back across the table with the flat of her hand. Anyone who bets what they love to be taken seriously, she says, has earned the truth test instead. So tell her the odds of this venture, exactly as you reckon them.

choices.label: Tell her the odds, bad and getting worse.
Snatch the sword back and call the dice crooked.

nodes.body: You snatch the sword back and call the dice crooked, and the accusation lands on the one honest roll of the whole evening. The room turns its shoulders on you in a single motion, the way rooms do. Sable is already gone through some door nobody saw. Come morning, no scout in Barrowmere will walk east beside a welsher, and the writ finds steadier company.

You put the question to Elowen herself instead of over her head, which apparently nobody has tried. She answers Aldous quiet and square, with her chin up. She is going, master, with leave or without it, and better with. Because the hills are warded, and the wardens are gone, and somebody who actually listens has to go and look. The old man blinks his milky eyes, and the argument shifts under everyone's feet like a barge coming loose.

choices.label: Stand with her and let her words finish it.
Undercut her with a joke to ease the old man.

nodes.body: You ask what head start the Redcloaks have, and Hedda's answer comes back dry as a creek in August. Vane marched his porters up the high road at first light, she says, making noise enough to warn every crow in the county of his coming. So the fast ways are spoiled and spoken for. If you have any sense between the four of you, the quiet ways will matter more than the quick ones.

choices.label: Take the road with that in mind.

nodes.body: An old shrine keeper is sweeping the toll shrine steps as though the bell were still there to serve, and he trades his last winter apples for news of town with shameless efficiency. He pays better than he charges. The crow funnel hangs over the second fallow, he says, two days steady now. And at night he hears carts on the drover track, heavy ones, moving without lamps, that never once pass his shrine going home.

choices.label: Thank him and cut for the crow fields.
Ask him about the keep on the hill.

nodes.body: His voice drops although the road is empty for a mile in both directions. Harrowstone was sealed for reasons, he says. His own grandfather carried mortar for the warden order when they closed it, and came home changed and church quiet. If lights burn green up there now, then somebody is reading books that should have stayed chained to their shelves. He takes up his broom again as if the conversation had never happened.

choices.label: Carry that to the crow fields.

nodes.body: The shepherd's shortcut turns out to be a shortcut for shepherds, who know which folds of the downs are lies. Night catches the party in one of them, with a turned ankle and a cold rain coming sideways. You limp back to the toll shrine by dawn, a full day older, and the trail a full day colder. Some doors do not stay open while you find your way to them.

Weck's grandnephew mends eel traps on the landing and will talk for a copper, which he does at flood volume until his uncle glares. The caravan never came down to the water, he says. But three nights back, something big and quiet poled downriver in the dark, no lights, riding low the way a boat rides when it carries stone. Or coffins, he adds cheerfully, because boys of that age always add the coffins.

choices.label: Board the barge and watch the water with new eyes.

nodes.body: You lean far out over the rail to read the strange wake, and the rail is greasy with eel work, and the river has waited all season for exactly this. The cold comes up like a shutting book. Weck's boathook finds your collar too late, and the story of your season closes in water the color of ledger ink.

You try the drover table's trick on the first waystone and count four painted teeth. The second stone carries five. By the third, Elowen is working it aloud, her chalk moving on her slate, because the count climbs as the track runs east and that is not decoration, that is a tally. Distance markers, she says slowly, counting down to something. Or counting up to it. Either way, somebody made arithmetic out of teeth, and arithmetic means intention.

choices.label: Follow the count east with fresh eyes.
Send word of the tally back by a passing shepherd first.

nodes.body: The shepherd takes the errand solemn as a magistrate, standing straighter than his crook. Elowen sketches the tally onto birch bark for Marshal Hedda, teeth per stone, stone by stone, with the company's initials scratched underneath like signatures on a will. Insurance of a kind. If the hills keep you, at least the count comes home to someone who can read it. The shepherd tucks it in his hat and turns west without another word.

choices.label: Turn east on the heels of the message.

nodes.body: Before anyone runs anywhere, Elowen reads the crows themselves, an old trick of her master's. Where they walk bold, she says, the dead are old news. Where they spiral shy and will not settle, something still moves. You follow her pointing finger across the wreck ring. Over the overturned lead wain, and only there, the crows spiral shy, wheeling and refusing to land, marking the one place in the field where something is still alive.

choices.label: Go straight to the lead wain.

nodes.body: You circle the wreck twice at bow range before closing, and the patience pays. The arrows are black fletched and cheap, loosed in a single volley from the ditch, gathered now like dropped quills. And in the ditch itself, neat lines of pressed grass show where the ambush bedded down for half a day, waiting, patient as winter. This was not a raid that happened upon a caravan. This was an appointment, kept.

choices.label: Close in on the lead wain with the picture whole.
Climb the ridge for a lookout of your own.

nodes.body: You climb the ridge for a lookout of your own, and the lookout you never marked has been watching you climb the whole way. One horn note rolls out over the fallow country, flat and wrong, and the long grass answers it from three sides at once. The field takes you the way it took the caravan, quickly and with practice, and the crows settle back down to their inspection behind you.

Elowen names what the splint will need against fever, willow bark and bog myrtle from the ditch line, and you gather it at a run while the light dies. She packs the wound and binds the leg while Finch narrates his own rescue to keep himself conscious, a scout's habit, reporting everything. By the time the stars come out his breathing has eased, and his eyes have cleared enough to hold yours steadily. Ask, they say. Ask me everything.

choices.label: Hear everything he knows once the fever eases.

nodes.body: The shelter option nobody loves is an old field barrow with a leaning door stone, standing in its ring of nettles like a patient argument for walking on. Inside it is dry, and quiet the way held breath is quiet. Elowen chalks the threshold, consults her three spells, and declares the place merely dead, not restless, which tonight is the very best word in her vocabulary. The storm grumbles closer while you decide.

choices.label: Sleep dry in the dead mans doorway.
Reconsider and take the cold hedge instead.
Wedge the door stone half shut for safety.

nodes.body: You sleep dry in the dead man's doorway, among polite bones that keep to their own end of the chamber. The storm spends itself on the door stone all night without an invitation. Dawn arrives through the crack like a coin slid under a door. The company rises rested and warmer than it has any right to be, one story richer, and Elowen leaves a honey cake on the threshold, paying for the roof.

choices.label: Rise and take the drag trail.

nodes.body: In the small hours the door stone shifts, and not from inside. Small hands wedge it shut with fieldstone and laughter, quick work by things that know barrows. It takes two days to dig free, two days of scraped knuckles and careful air, and you surface into an empty field with the trail gone cold as the chamber behind you. Some shelters cost more than storms do.

Sable rigs her decoy beyond the hedges, and Josser's tin whistle earns its three coppers at last, skirling across the fields like a wounded hunt horn. The lamed driver takes the bait whole. It hobbles its mule around the bend to look, spear up, ears forward. For the length of one held breath the cage cart stands unattended in the sunken lane, and the boy inside has already worked one hand through the withies.

choices.label: Cut the boy out in the gap.
Take the mule and cart too for the wounded.
Whistle the hunt call for a bigger distraction.

nodes.body: The hunt call comes off the whistle one note too true. A real horn answers from two fields over, flat and falling, and then come feet, many feet, the escort turning back early at the run. The lane floods with spears while you are still in the hedges, and there is nothing to do but run west empty handed, with the boy's eyes following you through the withies all the way out of sight.

The drain chain at the causeway's end holds your weight, and you come up onto the gatehouse roof among slates loose as bad teeth. A murder hole breathes torch smoke up from the guard room below, warm and rank. Through the shimmer, the whole inner ward lies laid out upside down for study, fires and kennels and the hay wain, geography worth more than any coin you have carried tonight.

choices.label: Cross the slates to the wall walk.
Listen at the murder hole first.
Cross the slates at a hunters trot.

nodes.body: You cross the slates on all fours with the wind helping badly, testing each one before it takes your weight, and you gain the wall walk in the gap between sentry rounds. From there a stair runs down into the outer ward's shadow. It was built for defenders in a hurry, and it stands empty tonight. You take it one quiet tread at a time, and Harrowstone accepts you onto its stones without noticing.

choices.label: Take the stair down into the ward.

nodes.body: The murder hole delivers the guard room's gossip along with its smoke, and you lie flat on the slates and collect all of it. The ash robed one pays moon by moon, and the pay is running late. The diggers hate the deep levels, where they swear the stone knocks back at them. And the password for the wicket tonight is bone price, spoken twice, which to anyone lying on a roof is worth more than a ladder. You file it away carefully and pick your descent.

choices.label: Down the inner chain to the ward, password in pocket.
Try the wicket boldly with the password.

nodes.body: One slate leaves under your boot and rings off the causeway stones below like a struck shield. The guard room empties upward through the roof hatch with commendable speed, and the drain chain becomes a bell rope with you on it. You ride it down into a ring of raised spears and torchlight, and the cells collect four more tenants who arrived by the roof.

You wade into the arch shadows under the causeway itself. Pillar to pillar, the bog breathes around your knees, and the dice game rattles somewhere overhead. It is slow going and cold going. But no eyes ever come down here, because nothing sane comes down here. Ahead through the forest of wet stone, the gate footing rises out of the water like a black altar. It is climbable, if your fingers hold.

choices.label: Climb the footing masonry to the wicket.

nodes.body: You climb the footing on mason marks worn soft by two centuries of weather, up past the level of the toll brazier glow. You cross the parapet lip in the gap between sentries and come down at last into the arch shadow of the gatehouse itself. Your arms are finished and your clothes are bog to the waist. But you stand inside the toll now, and the wicket waits three strides away.

choices.label: Slip through into the outer ward.

nodes.body: Sable's fingers find tool marks in the cliff where no tools should be, and the marks unfold into a wonder. An old warden stair, steps cut inside a chimney of rock, hidden from every angle that matters. A century of jackdaw nests chokes it nearly solid, but under the muck the steps climb sound and secret, patient as the order that cut them. Somebody wanted a quiet way up this cliff, and built one to last.

choices.label: Clear as you climb, slow and hidden.
Climb fast and let the nests fall.

nodes.body: Nest muck and patience, handful by handful, and the chimney gives you passage a step at a time. It tops out inside a roofless turret on the north wall itself, open to the stars. The jackdaws resettle around you with mild professional interest, as if the wardens had never left and you were simply late. From the turret, the breach is a short walk along an empty parapet.

choices.label: Walk the parapet to the breach and drop in.

nodes.body: You climb fast and the nests go down the cliff face in a black avalanche of outraged birds, a century of housing collapsing in one screaming minute. Every jackdaw on the hill takes up your cause and broadcasts your position to the watch fires, thoroughly and at volume. The slingstones start before the birds settle. The climb surrenders under the bombardment, and the hills stay shut.

You wait out the deep watch in a cliff hollow, cold soaking up through the stone into your bones a coin at a time. It is a long, mean, patient wait. But the sentry rounds thin as the night deepens, down to one bored walker, and then the moon drops behind the broken tower and takes the light with it. The last pitch waits in the dark gap, and the dark gap is yours.

choices.label: Climb the last pitch in the dark gap.
Search the cliff hollow while you wait.

nodes.body: You sound the pipe mouths by knuckle tap before wading, the way well diggers read stone before trusting it. Dead thud on the left mouth, and a live echo on the right, long and hollow, running deep beneath the wall. The drain maps itself in knocks while the water listens, and the true run picks itself out by ear alone. Bram nods slowly, professionally impressed, and the company goes in on the strength of a sound.

choices.label: Follow the live echo to the inner grate.

nodes.body: You improvise a dam from the fallen grate and armfuls of bog turf, and the culvert flow drops to ankle deep in a hundred breaths. The walk in becomes half as cold and twice as quiet, water noise fading to a trickle. Behind you the dammed pool spreads slow across the ditch, patient, gathering weight against your engineering with the whole night ahead of it.

choices.label: Walk the lowered channel to the inner grate.
Hurry before the dam decides otherwise.
Follow the otter run you spot while damming.

nodes.body: The dam decides otherwise at the worst turn. A pulse of black water comes down the pipe like a swallowed shout, taking knees and lantern oil and nerve all together. The drain flushes the party back into the ditch like leaves, soaked and sputtering, directly under the eyes of the wall watch above. The horns start before you finish standing up, and the night ends running.

You shout once up the pipe to test the echo, and the echo answers twice, once in stone and once in goblin. The inner grate slams somewhere ahead with a boom that rolls down the pipe past your ears, and the drain becomes a stone net drawing shut around you, spears at the ditch mouth and lamplight wading in from both ends. You are collected wet, and the cells do not improve matters.

You cross the dell kiln to dead kiln, a game of statues played in soot. The soot masks faces and smell alike, until the whole party is charcoal colored and next to invisible. From an oven's shadow you study the cart tunnel mouth at leisure while a dig crew trundles bones out into the night. You count their rhythm and learn the gap between crews. When you finally slip inside, it is on their own schedule.

choices.label: Slip into the cart tunnel behind the crew.

nodes.body: Mag has a rival, it turns out. A wire thin young burner with singed brows names himself Coalfinger and offers a quicker way into the hill for the price of your spare boots. His grin is hungry and his tunnel is real enough, you can see its mouth from here, propped like an afterthought by somebody who learned engineering from watching. It is quick, he repeats. He does not repeat the word safe.

choices.label: Pay the boots and take his quick way.
Refuse him with a laugh and walk on.

nodes.body: Coalfinger's way is quick as promised and mean as feared. Hands and knees under props that click like shaken dice, breathing charcoal by the spoonful, while he chatters cheerfully the whole crawl, which somehow does not help at all. You come out at last into the junction under the spoil chute, filthy and owing him boots. He salutes with them already under his arm and vanishes back into his own bad engineering.

choices.label: Climb the spoil chute into the ward.

nodes.body: Coalfinger takes the refusal with a shrug and sells the next best thing he owns, which is you. His whistle brings the dig crew at a trot while he counts his finder's fee on his fingers, and the dell fills with picks and lamplight from three tunnel mouths at once. Mag watches from her kiln as they take you, and her face says she has seen this trade before.

Your trade pidgin proves thinner than the soot on your face. The crew foreman listens, tilts its head, and asks one question in real goblin, slow and courteous. The silence after it is very long. The barrow ride to the cells is very bumpy, and the crew sings the shift song the whole way, which under other circumstances you would have found useful.

The ward smithy is banked for the night, all shadow and cooling iron and the smell of quenched steel. A path threads through it toward the inner door, lined both sides with tool racks. And one goblin smith sleeps stretched on the anvil itself with a hammer for a pillow, snoring like a bellows with a leak. The racks are close, the shadows are deep, and the sleeper holds the only alarm that matters in his dreaming hand.

choices.label: Thread the racks past the sleeper.
Muffle his hammer hand with a rag first, then thread.
Feel through the dark racks at speed.

nodes.body: Your elbow finds the one hanging tong chain in all that dark. And iron applause rings off every rack in the smithy. The smith comes awake mid snore with his hammer already moving, and the smithy door fills with torches before the echoes settle. Caught among the tools of somebody else's trade, you are marched to the cells with the applause still ringing in your ears.

The ward well stands in its corner with a windlass rope sound as the day it was wound, drum greased, handle willing. You ride it down past wet stone courses into the dark, and at half depth a ledge opens exactly where Finch's map promised. The spring gallery, a forgotten service way running under the inner door, dry as old bone and quiet as the inside of a bell.

choices.label: Ride the windlass down and take the spring gallery.
Trust the windlass at full speed.

nodes.body: The windlass pawl shears at half depth with a crack like a knuckle, and the rope screams through the drum. The bucket ledge arrives hard and wet, knocking the wind out of the whole night. The long climb back up delivers you into waiting torchlight, wrung out and dripping, and the well keeps your dignity as a deposit while the guards collect the rest.

You try the wicket boldly, bone price, spoken twice, and the password turns out to be a night stale. Blank yellow stares answer you, and then horns answer the stares. The gate arch fills from both ends while you stand there with yesterday's key in your mouth, and the walk back down the causeway is long, bright, and supervised all the way to the cells.

For one grasping moment you choose the pack strap over the next handhold, and the cliff takes its plain price for divided attention. There is no malice in it. Mountains do not do malice, only arithmetic. The heather far below keeps the rest of the season, and the pack, for whatever comfort that is, lands beside you with everything intact.

You take the third side mouth on a guess to save the recounting, and the guess narrows, and narrows, until your elbows pin against wet stone and panic starts knocking at the inside of your ribs. They dig you out backward by the ankles into the ditch, scraped and gasping. The night is spent, and the nerve is spent with it, and the drain keeps its dark to itself.

You follow the diggings one gallery too deep on the smell of old air. And the lamplight ahead proves to be a returning crew with empty barrows and full hands. There is no side pocket wide enough this time, and no dark deep enough either. They walk you up out of the galleries in procession, pleased with the find, and the deep levels keep the rest of their secrets behind you.

The hay wain rolls past the inner arch without slowing, and by the time the wrongness of that lands, it is parking at the kennel gate. Pitchforks come first at the kennels, as pitchforks always do, and the hay gives up its stowaways to a ring of grinning handlers and very interested dogs. The ride to the cells is short, and everyone itches the whole way.

The laundry vats steam faintly off the west passage, cold gray water and lines of goblin wash hung like surrendered flags. Behind them threads the servants' way, a passage narrow as a conscience. Running behind the guard nook where nobody with a spear has ever bothered to look. You duck under the wash lines and take it, smelling of soap and safe passage, the argument in the nook fading behind the wall.

choices.label: Thread the servants way to the block corner.
Steal the water round, buckets, yoke, and all.

nodes.body: Sable goes to the kennel run with a wink and a strip of jerky. And two hundred heartbeats later the ward above erupts in honest dog joy at a sprung gate. Boots thunder the wrong way across the cobbles, whistles and curses and the delighted barking of dogs who cannot believe their luck. The cell block stands suddenly unwatched, and the gift of noise is yours for exactly as long as the chase runs.

choices.label: Open the doors in the gift of noise.
Wait out the noise before touching a door.

nodes.body: You wait out the noise one round too long, and the kennel chase loops back early with the dogs leading it home. Wet noses find the block corner unerringly, joyful and thorough, and the whole west wing arrives behind them at a run. The dogs are extremely pleased with everyone involved. The feeling is not mutual, and the cells close over the rescue like water.

The drain grate in the block floor lifts on Bram's fingers a knuckle at a time, patient as tide. Below runs a dry way, low and close, threading under the cell row itself. You go along it tapping softly beneath each door, mapping which cells breathe and which stand empty. And the block answers you in shifting straw and held coughs, an inventory taken from underneath while the warder dreams over his keys.

choices.label: Come up inside the block past the warder entirely.

nodes.body: You split the party against every instinct you own. You and Sable to the gallery, light and fast. Bram and Elowen holding the block, shield and craft. Two smaller shadows instead of one loud one, and the meeting point named twice over so nobody can mishear it. Bram grips your shoulder once, hard, which is a whole speech in dwarf. Then the wing swallows the halves of you separately.

choices.label: Go light and fast to the court gallery.

nodes.body: You seat the cistern lid a finger too true, and the dark under it goes close, and closer, and spends itself. The child cries at last at the shut black, one thin wail through stone, and the lid comes up into lamplight and leveled spears. The hiding becomes the finding, and the block refills in the gray hours, one cough richer than before.

You whistle the block an all clear a shade too tunefully, and the warder's own whistle answers it from the chair, which was the real signal all along. The block corner becomes the best lit place in the west wing before you can lower your fingers from your lips. Old traps in old prisons, and the oldest one is confidence.

You light the block lamps to work faster, and the light works faster than you do. It shines like a festival through every grate and crack in the wing, and the passage guards come to it moth sure, spears first. The freeing is undone by its own light, and the dark you should have kept comes back down over everything like a dropped lid.

You cross upright through the lamp spill while the argument peaks, and the argument ends at exactly that moment, the way arguments do. Two guards and one silence agree completely on what they are looking at. The west wing folds shut around the shout like a book closing on a fly, and the cells collect you with the lamp still swinging.

The minstrel boxes hang above the hall's flank, dust sheeted and forgotten since the keep last owned music. From the first box the sleeping windrows spread below like a lord's eye view of a strange harvest. Box to box runs a creeping road the length of the hall, railed, shadowed, and untraveled for a century. The floors, of course, are another question, and each one asks it separately.

choices.label: Creep the boxes to the dais end.
Test each box floor before trusting it.
Trot the boxes without testing the floors.

nodes.body: The last box overhangs the dais itself, close enough to read the war banner's stitching. Below waits the plunder heap, and behind it the fresh lit passage. The drop is a body's length onto hay bales that were somebody's bedding once, soft enough if taken right. You hang, breathe, and let go, and the dais end of the hall receives you in a puff of dust and old straw.

choices.label: Drop to the dais and take the landing.

nodes.body: One box floor is older than its neighbors, and it chooses your weight to prove it. You go through in a shriek of nails into the pantry below, floundering among flour sacks and pickled eels while the hall wakes hungry on the other side of the wall. The midden door takes you out white as ghosts and empty handed, and the flour trail marks your retreat all the way to the wall.

The strongroom under the dais opens to Sable's picks and its own newer lock, which was the tell. Inside sits Vessk's private hoard, trade silver and better, and a drover's strongbox unopened. Wedged behind it all lean the caravan's missing ledgers, every name and consignment, proof written in Tallow's own hand. Worth more than the silver to nine families, and it weighs nothing at all in the pack.

choices.label: Take the ledgers and go for the court gallery.
Force the strongbox too while you are here.

nodes.body: The strongbox hasp screeches like a gutted hinge, iron on iron, and the dais overhead creaks awake in answer. The strongroom proves exactly what it was built to be. A room that holds things until their owner comes, and the owner comes with friends. You are collected among the silver like part of the inventory, and booked into the cells with rather less ceremony.

You tip the quench trough into the banked coals, and steam rolls out white and outraged, filling the armory corridor wall to wall. Iron hisses like a yard of geese. Inside the cloud every shape is just weather to a watching eye, and you walk out through your own fog at a steady pace, dripping and invisible, toward the court stair beyond.

choices.label: Walk out in the steam toward the court stair.
Linger in the armory for one more armload first.

nodes.body: You linger in the armory for one more armload, and the steam thins at the corridor mouth exactly as the smiths return from supper. Outlines resolve into strangers carrying the shop. Tongs and hammers take up the question, and the armory answers it with numbers, and the cells collect what the steam no longer hides.

The whole tablet stack proves heavier than curiosity and slicker than wax has any right to be. It goes over at the worst arch with a clatter like dropped crockery, and the plunder heap acquires guards with nothing else to do all night. You leave empty handed at a run, and the delivery note stays behind with all its unread cousins.

You help yourself to the forge tongs in passing, pure farm habit, and their chain follows them off the hook. The rack follows the chain, the corridor follows all of it, and the smiths arrive back from supper to find the cause standing in the wreckage still holding the evidence. The cells have a blacksmith joke about it by morning, and you hear every version.

You carry the oil uncorked for speed, and speed proves the enemy. A slick heel turn at the service stair, a lamp spark, and the corridor lights up with you inked black at the center of it. The hunt is short and bright. Driven out through the midden with your shadow burned onto the wall behind you, you leave the keep roused and the night ruined.

You read the incense drift like river water, eddies at the niche mouths. A steady pull under the painted curtain where the den breathes it in. Then you light a heavier pot of your own and feed the flow until the whole east passage swims gray. Elowen manages the burn, eyes streaming, while the smoke thickens into cover you can walk inside, slow as weather and about as suspicious.

choices.label: Move inside the drift to the curtain.

nodes.body: The drift becomes a gray river, and you four are stones in it, moving only when it moves. The novice tends its pots an arm's length away without seeing anything but smoke doing what smoke does. The curtain edge arrives like a bank looming out of fog, hide and painted teeth suddenly close enough to touch. And the river carries you against it and holds you there, breathing shallow.

choices.label: Take the curtain edge.
Hold in the deepest drift though your chest heaves.

nodes.body: You hold in the deepest drift though your chest heaves, and the chest wins, as chests do. One smothered cough at the curtain edge, small as a mouse, and the drone stops mid note. The curtain sweeps back on a shaman that was only ever pretending to scry, eyes bright and waiting, and the den collects you out of your own smoke like fruit from a bowl.

The novice's bone flute lifts from its belt loop in passing, light fingered and unnoticed. Three notes, learned from a night's listening at the wall. The summons that empties the den when the shaman wants privacy, played soft and hoped hard. Inside, the droning stops. Feet shuffle. The curtain swings, and the shaman stalks out past your niche trailing smoke and irritation, gone to answer a call nobody sent.

choices.label: Play the summons and slip in as the den empties.
Play it loud to be sure it carries.

nodes.body: The third note comes out a shade flat, and the difference between privacy and alarm is apparently one half tone. The den empties in the wrong direction, outward, at speed, with teeth. The east wing arrives to explain the distinction at spear point. And the flute is confiscated by a novice who looks, briefly and unprofessionally, delighted.

You name the shaman's true name as the debt, and the crow spirit laughs like dropped slates. Names are not debts, it says, and neither is rescue, now. The cage stands empty, the debt is spent on the asking, and the den is awake around you with the shaman's drone rising into something with edges. The spirit watches the capture from the rafters, curious, taking notes in its fashion.

One question too many for the bound shaman, whose answer is a bitten thumb and a sign smeared on the floor in its own blood. The wards wake wall by wall like lamps being lit at dusk, sinew tightening, teeth turning. The east wing folds in while you are still backing away from the smear, and the shaman, trussed and gagged, contrives to look satisfied the entire time.

You pocket a tally chalk in passing, a souvenir, a nothing. But the count misses it within the hour, because small things are what foremen are for. The doors shut before the next basket moves, the floor is searched sorter by sorter, and the strangers with the chalk in their pocket are sorted last and hardest. The cells take you still holding it.

You drop to the court floor while the last captains still argue, and a gallery shadow arrives among them like a delivered parcel. The war court stops arguing. It is very pleased to have something to agree on at last, and what it agrees on is you. The rite gains a witness in irons, and the gallery keeps the lesson.

You send Sable ahead alone, because she asks with her hands on her hips, and she is back in a hundred breaths with the layout chalked on her own palm. Drummers here, trestle there, ranks so, and a warden's postern seam in the wall that she cannot open but thinks you might. Then her flat advice, delivered eye to eye. Do not be on the floor when the singing starts.

choices.label: Move on the antechamber with her map in mind.

nodes.body: You swing wide on the rope to skip the last stone courses, and the rope pays out one fray too far. It parts at the whipping with a sound like a snapped lute string. A ledge breaks the drop, and the climb back up breaks what is left of your pride. The well is abandoned to the torch answers gathering above, and the way down is finished for the night.

You surf the spoil heap like a hay slide, and for a breath and a half it is glorious. Then the heap arrives at the antechamber floor and you arrive in it, buried to the waist in bones at the feet of the door watch. Many small unfriendly hands dig you out, none of them gentle, and the rite gains a front row audience in irons.

You borrow a sconce torch against the drain dark and carry it like a confession down the boneyard passage. At the first turning, every eye the deep owns answers the light, wide and yellow and interested. You douse it far too late to be unseen. The dark that follows is full of soft feet, and the cells are full by midnight, you among them.

A warden's armory niche opens off the hidden passage, gear of the order racked a century deep in dust. Rotted straps, green bronze, spear heads gone to lace. And one horn of pale wound metal that the dust has not touched at all, hanging apart, as if the years agreed to leave it be. You take it down with a warden's prayer you improvise on the spot, and it settles against your hip like it has been waiting.

choices.label: Carry the wardens horn to the doors blind side.

nodes.body: You join the last rank of the procession at a mourner's shuffle, heads down among goblin backs, borrowed cloaks reeking of tallow smoke. The column carries you toward the trestle the way a river carries driftwood, no effort asked, none offered. Drums walk the pace. Nobody looks at anybody in a rite, which is the one mercy of ceremonies, and the lamp ring glow grows ahead of you stride by stride.

choices.label: Peel off into the pillar shadows at the lamp ring.
Ride the column all the way to the trestle.

nodes.body: You meet the honor guard captain eye to eye with the horn held plain in both hands. A veteran with a torn ear, he listens to three sentences of truth without blinking, which is more than most humans manage. Then he jerks his chin toward Vessk and steps aside. The chief hears mad things himself tonight, he says in trade tongue, and one more can join the queue.

choices.label: Put the truth to Vessk under the captains truce.
Offer the captain silver to clear your way.

nodes.body: You join the relief drummers' line at the carry, a skin drum shouldered like a sack of turnips, chin tucked, elbows in. The line trots the gallery stair in step, and the balcony arrives around you from inside the rhythm itself, smoke and thunder and sweating backs. Nobody counts heads in a relief line. The drums do all the counting tonight, and they only count beats.

choices.label: Set the drum down and drift to the far stair.
Keep the drum and play along in the line.
Shoulder a second drum to look busier.

nodes.body: You join your voice to Elowen's counter chant on the second phrase, tuneless but stubborn, and two voices make the wrong note bigger than one. The seal glow gutters like a candle in a doorway. The shaman's song tears down the middle, half of it chasing your voice, half clinging to the verse, and holding neither. Elowen's hand finds your sleeve in the dark and grips it, and the chant walks forward toward the trestle.

choices.label: Press through the dying song toward the trestle.

nodes.body: You kick the burning oil in a bright fan across the processional rugs, and flame walks up the embroidered kings at a stroll. Goblins abandon rank for water and blame in equal measure. The trestle stands alone in a widening ring of light, unguarded because everything near it is on fire, which is a kind of guarding, but not against you.

choices.label: Take the box straight off the trestle in the glare.
Herd the flames wider before you move.

nodes.body: The fire keeps its own counsel. A gust off the door gap folds the flame sheet back across your line, and you come out of it singed and smoked, coughing, in the open. The ranks close around you like a purse, and the rite is delayed one night by the damage, no more, and the cells keep the party warm while the rugs are replaced.

You take the drummers' stair against the tide of routing goblins, up where everything else is going down. And burst out onto the keep's broken roofline with the box against your ribs. The night wind is astonished to see you. Below, Harrowstone empties itself in torch streams from every gate, and the rooflines run north toward the breach, silver under the setting moon, a road nobody else wants tonight.

choices.label: Run the rooflines north and down the breach.

nodes.body: Roofline to wall walk to breach, and then the hills at a hunter's jog under the setting moon. You loop wide of every goblin line of march for two days, cold camps and stolen turnips and watches shared out in whispers. The box rides quiet on your back, as if it approves of patience. And on the third dawn the smoke of Barrowmere's chimneys comes up over a hill like a promise kept.

choices.label: Come home the long way.

nodes.body: You pry at the deepest carving for the crown behind it, and the warden's masonry answers exactly as built. A soft fall of counterweighted stone seals the passage both ways, patient as a mousetrap a century in the setting. Puzzled goblins dig you out at dawn into custody, the rite long over. Merely postponed, and the wardens, wherever they are, unsurprised.

Bone dust and one held sneeze, and on the ninth landing the sneeze wins its argument. It echoes down the long stair like a war horn of the nose, impossible to mistake for anything else. The pillar shadows empty of everything but goblins, and the stair delivers you to the floor by the shortest route, under escort, with your eyes still watering.

The honor guard captain weighs your silver with genuine sorrow. Then he takes it, and you, both. A bribe is property, he explains in careful trade tongue, and property goes to the chief. The queue for Vessk's justice is long tonight, and the rite is entirely visible from it, which the goblins consider a kindness to prisoners with nothing else to watch.

The relief line steps on the offbeat you supplied, and the stumble runs up the whole file drum by drum, a wave of ruined time. The captain wheels with his knotted cord already singing. The balcony becomes a very small room with one door, and the door is not on your side of the room, and neither is anything else.

The third call is one call too many. The panic curdles into pattern, captains triangulating the vaults with torch lines, methodical as surveyors. The horn answers its own echo in a room already full of listeners. And the listeners are very interested in meeting the player, and the meeting is not long delayed.

The second lashing parts with a groan the drums cannot cover, and the scaffold sags like a tired horse. The drum captain follows the sound to its source with his cord coiled, unhurried, because the gallery holds everything it catches and it has just caught you. The scaffold, for the record, holds all night.

You widen the postern crack one finger for a better view, and the view improves in both directions. The shaman's eye meets yours down the gap for a heartbeat that lasts a season. Then the blind side is blind no more, and small hard hands are already at the postern seam, and the warden's passage gives up its last secret along with its guests.

You take the carved face's front in the open to save a scramble, and your silhouette prints on the lamp glow for the whole floor to read. The descent finishes in a forest of raised spears grown up around the chin. You are helped down the king's nose with more ceremony than kindness, and the rite gains its audience.

You pause to wrap the humming box mid run, and the hum swells under the cloth like a hive disturbed. Its one note calls down the corridors clear as a bell, to everything that hunts by ear tonight. They run you down within sight of the drain, the box singing cheerfully in your arms the whole way, loyal to no one.

The bound wizard is paraded through the ward for the freed to see, and one gloating circuit becomes two. His lips move inside the gag on the second lap. The sigil at his throat flares white, and the rope is suddenly holding nothing but warm air and a smell of ash. The night is still won, the people still free, the box still yours. But the ledger is left open, and somewhere south a debt is walking.

The road shrine inside the gate is small and bright, crowded with wax stubs and travelers' tokens. An old porter feeds the lamp with a steady hand and nods at your sword the way some men nod at weather. The roads have been eating people since the wardens guttered out, he says comfortably, and here is the joke of it, nobody living even remembers what the wardens warded. The lamp flame leans, steadies, and seems to wait there with you.

choices.label: Light a stub for luck and ask him about the wardens.
Light a stub and go in to the warmth.
Stay through the lamp trimming to hear the whole tale.

nodes.body: The porter's answer proves worth the wax. The warden order kept the old places quiet, he tells you, barrows and doors and deep places that wanted watching, paid in king's silver until the kings stopped paying and the order guttered out like a lamp. He trims the wick without hurry. Their last chapterhouse stood somewhere out east, he thinks, in the Hollow Hills if his grandfather told it true. He glances at you sideways and wishes you a good sleep, in the voice of a man who doubts you will get one.

choices.label: Carry that into the inn.

nodes.body: The shrine lamp proves better company than courage, and you stay through the trimming and one more story after that. The curfew bell catches you still praying, and town law bars the inn until dawn. The muster happens at first light on the wrong side of a locked gate, and then the east road carries every sound of it away.

Pip the pot boy will trade the kitchen's whole view of the world for a copper and the glory of being consulted. The interesting part comes last. The Redcloaks bought no rations for the road, he says, not so much as a loaf, and even a pot boy knows what that means. They intend to turn back rich, or they never intend to go far at all. He pockets the copper like a state secret and returns to his kettles.

choices.label: Take that thought to the clerk.

nodes.body: Pell keeps survey maps unrolled under the lamp for anyone with the wit to look. The Hollow Hills spread across the parchment in faded ink, and there on the second ridge stands Harrowstone, drawn carefully with its double wall. Along the east face, in a surveyor's spidery hand, run three small words that may be worth more than the whole fifty crowns. Drains east under. You read them twice and fold the knowledge away where nobody can pick your pocket of it.

choices.label: Commit the map to memory and go find the dwarf.

nodes.body: The vigil grandmother finds you one last time at the gate and presses honey cakes wrapped in a clean kerchief into Elowen's hands, because provisions are what grandmothers command instead of armies. She names her granddaughter once more, Berta, eleven years old, among the taken. Then she asks for nothing at all, out loud, which of course asks for everything. You march east with the kerchief knot riding the top of the pack like a small white flag.

choices.label: Promise her nothing aloud and everything else, and march.

nodes.body: The second farmstead has burned days more recently than the first, smoke still ghosting up from the byre in thin gray threads. Sable circles it once and whistles low. Down the well house steps, two points of light resolve into eyes, and the eyes into an old couple, alive and hidden and half starved, watching you the way mice watch a new cat. It takes bread and patience to coax them up into the daylight.

choices.label: Coax them up and hear what they saw.
Leave food and press on hard for the crow fields.
Track the cart dust cross country while it hangs.

nodes.body: Their story comes out between mouthfuls, in pieces. Cage carts have rolled east three nights running, small drivers cursing in no honest tongue, wheels heavy in the lane mud. And beside the last cart walked a man in gray robes, reading as he went, by no lamp that either of them could see. The old woman crosses her fingers against the memory. Her husband just watches the east hills and chews.

choices.label: Set them on the road to the toll shrine keeper and go on.

nodes.body: You point the old couple west with the last of the bread and the shrine keeper's name for shelter, and they spend their blessing on you like the final coin in a purse, thoroughly and with both hands. Then the lane is empty and the morning is spending down. Ahead over the fallow country the crow funnel turns and turns, marking the place the fields keep hidden.

choices.label: Make for the crows.

nodes.body: You chase the hanging cart dust cross country on fresh anger, and the country wins, the way it always wins. Hedges and ditches eat the miles you meant to save, one gate at a time. The party staggers into the crow fields a day late and spent to the boots, and the trail there has aged past honest reading. Anger is a poor compass, and the proof of it costs you the season.

The weir walk runs below the landing, hurdles and plank ways over sliding black water. Weck names his toll for the shortcut without shame. One hour's honest work lifting eel traps, each one heavy as sin and twice as lively. The fog is rising off the river like a slow curtain, and the plank line runs away into it, slick and inviting and unpaid for.

choices.label: Pay the hour and lift traps.
Walk the plank line fast through the rising fog.

nodes.body: The plank line in fog is a lesson in humility. One slick board turns underfoot, and the weir pool takes you, cold as charity. Eel men haul you out and wring you out on the bank, kind and unhurried and full of advice. The day drains away west with the trail in it, and the company sleeps wet in Weck's boat shed, one shortcut poorer.

Traps lifted and toll paid, Weck's tongue loosens with the shared work, and three nights back, he says, a low boat poled downriver in the dark, quiet as guilt, riding heavy. And on its stern rail, caught in his lantern for one blink, teeth painted red as fresh paint. He spits into the weir pool and waits to see what you make of it.

choices.label: Ask what a goblin wants with a boat.

nodes.body: Weck's shrug is worth a chapter. Goblins hate water, he says, hate it like cats do. So whatever rode that boat mattered more than the hate, heavy and boxed and bound downriver toward the marsh country, while every eye in the district watches the hills instead. You file it away where you keep the things that will matter later, and follow the crows west.

choices.label: File it away and follow the crows west.

nodes.body: The drover shrine sits in its hollow off the track, horn stakes and braided grass offerings layered generations deep, the kind of place shaped by a thousand small gratitudes. Dead center on the altar stone, wrong as a wasp in honey, lies a slate painted with a fresh red tooth. Nothing else is disturbed. It was not thrown there or dropped there. It was placed, square and deliberate, by something that understood exactly what the altar was for.

choices.label: Let Elowen read the tooth against the offerings.
Knock the slate off the altar and move on.
Follow the drum sound straight over the rise.

nodes.body: Elowen reads the tooth against the offerings, careful as stitchwork, and her conclusion comes out level. It is not desecration, she says. It is notice. Territory claimed, posted where drovers post their own signs, by something that has learned how signs work. Which means the Redtooth are thinking like herders now, with ranges and borders to keep. And something, she adds quietly, has given them a border worth keeping. The hollow holds its breath around the altar.

choices.label: Carry the thought east along the track.

nodes.body: You leave the hollow behind, but its echo keeps pace, because on the last rise the wind turns and brings you drums. Far off, patient, steady as a resting heart, keeping a time that no herd ever walked to. East, and low, as if the sound were coming up through the hills rather than over them. Sable listens with her head tilted, then lengthens her stride, and nobody needs the order repeated.

choices.label: Follow the track to the crow fields.

nodes.body: The drums pull, and you follow them straight over the rise on pure curiosity, which is exactly what the drums are for. The picket of small spears takes you in the dead ground beyond, folded around you before your sword clears leather, professional as tax collectors. They march you north through the dark, and the drums keep their patient time the whole way to the cells, counting you in.

Tallow's office wain lies on its side with its bills of lading snowed across the fallow. Under the seat sits a locked money box, heavy and smug, and nailed inside the lid you find the guard roster. Six names, written in Tallow's careful hand. You look back over the wreckage and count again, slower. Among all the scattered boots and bedrolls, there is gear for only four guards. Two men are missing from a fight that took everyone else.

choices.label: Track the two missing guards from the ditch line.
Force the money box first.

nodes.body: The money box answers the pry bar with a merchant's needle trap, one bright scratch, and by nightfall a numbness is climbing your arm like ivy. Elowen burns the fever out in a shepherd's hut over two lost days, swearing at merchants the whole time. The box was empty, of course, and the trail goes colder than the needle while your hands relearn steadiness.

The missing guards' trail is short and honest. The first lies at the hedge line where he fell, face to the enemy, and you bury him under fieldstone with his sword laid true along his side. The second tracked west to the road, running hard by the stride of it, and kept running. Nobody says the word coward out loud over a fresh grave. Bram says the drover's words instead, short and old, and you go back for the knocking.

choices.label: Say the drovers words and go back for the knocking.

nodes.body: The wagon jack comes off Tallow's office wain, drover engineering older than sieges, all thread and iron patience. You walk it under the wain bed and crank, and the whole broken world lifts one honest inch at a time, no heroics required. Finch watches the wain rise off him at the speed of arithmetic and starts grinning halfway through. Rescued by freight equipment, he says. The dignity of it. His voice cracks on the joke, and nobody minds.

choices.label: Splint him and hear everything he knows.

nodes.body: First watch falls to you and Bram, and the fire burns down to coals while neither of you says anything, which is how the dwarf prefers conversation. Then, unasked, the story under the scars comes out. A shieldwall broken at the Redwater, not by goblins but by a captain too proud to sound the retreat. That is why he only signs with people who admit they are afraid, he says. The fearless ones cost too much to bury.

choices.label: Keep the coals and the confidence till the watch turns.

nodes.body: Dawn watch belongs to Elowen, who names the constellations wrong on purpose until you argue with her. The Plow becomes the Spoon. The Herdsman becomes the Tax Collector. You last a quarter hour before correcting her, and she grins like a fisherman feeling the line pull. Arguing keeps sentries awake, she explains, entirely unashamed. She learned it from a book and improved it herself, and she has a whole sky's worth of ammunition left.

choices.label: Wake the others and take the drag trail.

nodes.body: By ember light, Sable teaches you knives, beginning with how to hold one so it cannot be taken from you, wrist locked and thumb flat along the grip. Then when to show it, which is early, so that it need never be used at all, which she says is the whole art. She owns exactly one rule she calls a rule, and she makes you repeat it twice into the dark. Never throw your last one. Your hand still remembers the lesson in the morning.

choices.label: Sleep on it and rise for the drag trail.

nodes.body: Tam walks beside you while the lane cools, fed now, working up to something through half a mile of silence. Then it comes, level as a plumb line, and he asks whether his father is alive, meaning Hobb, the lead drover of the caravan. The only honest answer is that you do not know and that you intend to find out, and he weighs that the way a grown man weighs coin before accepting it. Then he pays you back with the thing he hoarded all this time. The cell watch changes when the torch burns down to the painted ring.

choices.label: Send him south with word and turn northeast.

nodes.body: Pale lights drift off the causeway flank, hip high over the bog, like lanterns carried by nothing at all. They wander and pause and beckon, exactly the way lights should not. Elowen names them marsh gas in the voice of someone quoting a book she half believes. Nobody teases her for it. Out on the black water, one of the lights has just changed direction against the wind.

choices.label: Watch the lights until their pattern shows.
Follow the nearest light out onto the bog.

nodes.body: The nearest light stays always one step farther. The tussocks under your boots grow always one step fewer. By the time the trick is plain, the causeway is a rumor behind you. The bog closes its ledger the way it has closed a hundred before, cold and quiet and owed nothing. The lights drift on over the black water, patient, beckoning already to no one.

Sable stares the lights down until the true pattern surfaces. One lantern among the drifters burns steady, goblin made, and it swings a relay code from the gatehouse to the far shore, regular as a ferry timetable. Dark water, dark water, wicket open at the change. She reads it twice through to be sure, lips moving, and then grins the grin she saves for other people's locks.

choices.label: Time the wicket change and move on the gate.

nodes.body: You move on the gate inside the relay's own blind beat. Sable counts the change home under her breath like a gambler counting a shoe. The last stretch of causeway crosses at a fast walk, in exactly the interval when every eye that matters is reading a lantern somewhere else. The wicket shadow swallows the party whole. The toll brazier never looks up from its dice.

choices.label: Slip through into the outer ward.

nodes.body: A hawk's eyrie blocks the second pitch, sticks and sheep bones wedged in a cleft exactly where the path needs to go. The mother hawk mantles at the whole party, wings out wide, and lets loose a scream that could carry to the watch fires if she decides to keep it up. She has every advantage and knows it. Somebody is going to have to negotiate, and it is not going to be her.

choices.label: Let Elowen gentle her with slow hands and a strip of jerky.
Rob the eyrie for the eggs while she rages.

nodes.body: You rob the eyrie while she rages, and the mountain sides with the hawk, as mountains always will. Wings and talons and pure outrage drive the party back down ledge by ledge until the heather takes you. The climb is spent, the night is spent, and the eggs are not even good eating. Some tolls are not worth the shortcut, and this one had feathers.

Elowen gentles the hawk with slow hands and a strip of jerky, murmuring in the tone she uses on frightened things and stubborn masters. The bird settles to a furious simmer. One tiercel eye tracks her as she backs away, bowing like a courtier at a very difficult court. The pitch above the eyrie stays clean and quiet all the way to the wall. Nobody mentions how hard her hands shake afterward.

choices.label: Climb on to the broken wall.

nodes.body: Elowen starts her counting song low in the culvert mouth, a hedge school trick for dark places. Each verse marks a turning, each chorus counts the steps between, and the drain answers her in echoes that stack like shelves in the black. It should sound foolish, a girl singing arithmetic into a goblin drain. Instead it sounds like the first sensible thing anyone has done all night.

choices.label: Walk the song into the dark.

nodes.body: The song maps the drain as it goes, echoes shortening where side mouths open and lengthening where the run bends away. At the third verse her voice finds something better than geometry. Scratched numerals stand in the stone at shoulder height, warden survey marks, counting down toward the inner grate exactly as the old page promised. Somebody measured this dark once, carefully, and left the sums for whoever came after them.

choices.label: Follow the numerals to the grate chamber.
Sing the chorus louder for confidence in the dark.

nodes.body: The chorus goes up louder for confidence, and confidence finds an audience. The echo answers in goblin from the grate chamber ahead, sharp and delighted, and the drain becomes a stone throat with a shout at both ends of it. You back out soaked into waiting hands at the ditch mouth, and the song ends there, one verse short of the wall.

The numerals end at zero beside the inner grate, neat as a ledger ruled off. Its lock pin hangs by rust, and the wardens, who measured this door for their own returns once, clearly meant it to open for the right hands. It opens for yours. The stairs beyond breathe ward air down the pipe, woodsmoke and kennel and cold stone, the smell of the inside of the problem.

choices.label: Ease the grate and climb into the ward.

nodes.body: Mag tells her second story to the kilns more than to you, banking coals while she talks. Her grandmother burned charcoal for the warden order itself, she says, and the order paid to keep these tunnels open. Because a door you seal needs a back way for the sealers, and wardens always came in pairs. She looks up then, black eyes steady. Always in pairs, she repeats, as if counting your company against some older arithmetic.

choices.label: Ask her for the sealers back way.
Doubt her aloud and demand proof.

nodes.body: You doubt her aloud and demand proof, and Mag pays doubt in her own coin, and a shrug, and a poke at the kiln. Directions delivered with perfect courtesy that walk the party in a long patient circle through the diggings and back out to the dell mouth at dawn. The night is gone when you surface, and so is the old woman, kiln banked, string wound, gone entire. The hills stay shut.

She marks the sealers' way on your palm in charcoal, her hand steady as a beam scale. Third fork, high side, spoil chute, and then a word for the dark, free of charge. The wardens counted everything, she says, so count. She looks at each of you once more, and her eyes say plainly that she knows exactly what sleeps under this hill, and exactly what it costs to keep it sleeping, and that she has decided you will do.

choices.label: Take the sealers way to the spoil chute.

nodes.body: The east grating, up close at last, and behind the bars a face you recognize from a chalked manifest. Merchant Tallow grips the iron with both hands, his voice sanded down to a whisper. Nine alive, counting the child, and the child is fevering. Guards run thick on this wing until the deep drums call them down at moonset, he says. Then his hand takes your collar through the bars with surprising strength, and his one demand comes through the iron. Whatever else you do, the child goes out first.

choices.label: Promise it and take the midden door in.
Pass your spare knife through the bars first.

nodes.body: The spare knife is halfway through the bars when the corner torch rounds early, and iron rings on cobbles loud as a dropped bell. Tallow palms the blade even as the shout goes up, one small victory. It buys nothing for you. The ward closes in from both directions, and the party is taken pinned against the very grating it came to open, close enough to touch the people it failed.

Through the second cell door comes a sound that is not weeping, and you stop to parse it. A clapping rhythm, soft as moth wings, patterned, patient. The older drover children are teaching the little ones the quiet game, loudest whisper loses, and the whole block is keeping time under its jailers' noses. You crouch at the door and listen, and the rhythm carries something underneath it that sounds remarkably like order.

choices.label: Learn the rhythm before you touch the block.

nodes.body: You learn the rhythm through the door and tap it back once, soft, joining the game. The clapping answers in perfect time, and then holds, and you can feel nine people's worth of held breath behind the iron. The block is already organized. It has been organized for days, waiting for exactly you, or anyone like you, and the quiet game was never a game. It was a muster roll.

choices.label: Move on the cell block in the games own time.
Tap the rhythm again, eager, to be sure.

nodes.body: The second tap comes out one flourish too fond, pride in the rhythm, and the warder's chair creaks as his head tilts toward a sound that is not rats. Lamplight swells. The block wakes to shouts with the rhythm dead in the air, and the quiet game ends the way it always warned, loudest whisper loses, and the whisper was yours.

Behind the dais stair runs the trophy wall, shields and standards nailed up like hides from the border wars. A whole generation of plunder hangs there going dusty. Dead center is a round shield, scarred and unmistakable, painted with the boar of Bram's own old company. Beside you the dwarf's breath goes very slow and very even, which in Bram is the sound of a decision forming. His eyes do not leave the boar.

choices.label: Give Bram the moment he is owed.
Strip the whole wall of anything worth carrying.

nodes.body: You strip the wall greedy and wholesale, and the old timber objects. Nails sing loose, a standard pole rings off the dais flagstones like a dropped spear, and the hall wakes all at once to find its history walking out the door. It does not get far. Driven out through the midden empty handed, you learn exactly how fast a sleeping hall can stand up.

Bram lifts the boar shield down without one sound, which should not be possible for iron and old nails, and he crosses the scarred paint once with his thumb the way other men touch a grave marker. The shield goes onto his back where it has always belonged. When he turns around he is wearing a face you are glad is on your side. The way to the gallery landing feels shorter after that.

choices.label: Climb to the gallery landing with him.

nodes.body: The saints' crypt behind the boneworks holds warden burials, and Elowen reads the grave marks twice to be sure. Each skull rests pillowed on a chime of pale metal, one to a niche, rank on rank. Wardens sang in choirs, she says at last, very quietly. Always in choirs. One voice was never enough. The chimes wait in the dark under their skulls, holding their notes the way the dead hold everything, carefully.

choices.label: Take one wardens chime, with respect.
Pocket the grave silver while you are here.

nodes.body: You pocket the grave silver, and every niche mouth in the crypt grinds a hand's width shut at once, dust sifting down like a held sneeze. The boneworks beyond stirs at the sound of its dead objecting. You walk out of the east wing empty handed and marked, the silver returned in haste. The wards unforgiving, and the night poorer by one wing and one lesson about what belongs to whom.

You take one warden's chime from beneath its skull, with respect, both hands, the way you would take a tool from a sleeping craftsman. It is cold, and somehow willing, a note stored inside it like water in a jar. Elowen wraps it in wool against ringing early. If one voice is not enough, she whispers, we bring a warden's with us. The skull keeps its counsel, and its pillow is lighter.

choices.label: Carry the chime to the shamans den.

nodes.body: Tallow raises his right hand in the lamp dark and deputizes the entire cell block under freight law. Drovers, children, and you, sworn porters of one consignment, namely each other. The oath is older than marshals and twice as binding, he says, and nobody laughs, because nine backs straighten under it like masts taking sail. You sign the bond with your mark, and the block becomes a crew with a cargo.

choices.label: Sign the bond with your mark and set the signal.

nodes.body: Malverin's traveling desk stands in the shutter gap's narrow slice of view, and on it, beside the chained book, lies an open ledger in a fine hand. Sums paid to the Redtooth. Sums owed to him, by name after name after name. And one entry underlined at the foot of the page, in fresher ink. The Coil expects the door open by winter, and payment on the second seal. You carry the name down the stairs like a hot coal.

choices.label: Carry that name down toward the crypt stair.

nodes.body: Murals line the warden's passage where the spy slots pause. The choir stands painted at this same door in older robes, twelve singers with one raised hand. The door is shown shut, and behind it the crowned sleeper dreams a winter that fills the next three panels, white and total, herds dead in the furrows, rivers still. You take the lesson with you along the passage. The door was never a treasure house, only a lid.

choices.label: Take the lesson to the doors blind side.

nodes.body: The pillar forest floor offers its own road, bench stones and offering troughs giving belly cover between drumbeats. You go down among the gallery's own furniture and crawl, elbow and knee, while rank boots pass an arm's length away. Dust of centuries packs your sleeves. The drums cover everything except your own heart, which sounds, from down here, like somebody knocking to be let out.

choices.label: Crawl the last trough line to the lamp ring.
Crawl through the drum pause to save time.

nodes.body: The trough line ends in the trestle's own shadow, dust in your teeth and drums overhead like weather. The waking words begin close enough to feel on the back of your neck, dry syllables settling like frost. You gather your legs under you in the blind side dark and count the singer's breaths, one, two, and rise.

choices.label: Rise on the blind side and move on the rite.

nodes.body: You are one trough short of the shadow when the drums pause between verses. In the sudden hush your crawl sounds like a landslide. Small hard hands find your ankles before the next beat lands, and the trough line ends where the trestle's lamp ring begins, with you presented dusty and blinking to the rite you nearly reached.

In the stable yard a drover mare stands unclaimed with her nose deep in a feed bag. The ostler scratches her neck and explains that her owner has been two weeks silent, so he keeps her fed out of his own coppers. Somebody will come, he says, shrugging away his own kindness, they always come. The mare watches the east gate over the rail with the patience of furniture, and something in your chest pulls uncomfortably tight. You scratch her ears and carry the ache inside with you.

choices.label: Scratch her ears and carry the ache inside.
Take the unclaimed mare for one ride round the walls.

nodes.body: As you turn from the ledger, Pell speaks once more, quiet as a bookmark sliding into place. The marshal cannot say this, he murmurs, but I keep her accounts. Whatever is paying goblins in old silver has deeper pockets than a ruined keep full of rust should have. He dips his pen and does not look up again. Mind whose war you are walking into, he says, and the scratching of the nib follows you across the room.

choices.label: Mind it and go find the dwarf.

nodes.body: She takes the saddle sweetly, and for one lap of the walls you feel like a knight out of the old songs. Then the gate guard steps out with his hand raised, owning no sense of humor about horse theft at all. You spend a day explaining yourself to the watch, and the writ goes to steadier hands while you do.

You talk the commission over at one table too many, and by the second cider your route and your hour are common knowledge from the taps to the stable. At dawn the Redcloaks are lounging against the first milestone, saluting you with the head start you handed them. The marshal unsigns your name without one word. Somehow that stings worse than shouting.

You find the stolen bell after all, half buried in the ditch where the deserters dropped it for weighing more than its price. It takes four sets of hands to rehang it crooked on its yoke, and nobody explains out loud why the work feels necessary. Sable rings it once, softly, for the road. The note follows you east a surprising distance, thin and clean, like something small refusing to be finished.

choices.label: Leave it ringing behind you and make for the crows.

nodes.body: You walk the last stretch of the west bank instead of riding it, alder shade and heron stalk, reading the mud the way Sable taught you on the barge. It pays. In a mudbank smooth as a signature you find one soft shoed footprint among the goblin treads, days old, narrow, unhurried. It points downriver, toward the marsh country, while every other sign in the county points at the hills. You note it hard, the way you note a debt.

choices.label: Note it hard and follow the crows west.
