nodes.body: The last waystone before the fallow country carries eleven painted teeth, and you almost miss the rest. Under the count, ghosting through the rust red, older paint shows in the low light. A warden's mark, round and quartered, half devoured by the grin painted over it. The new border is eating the old one, Elowen says, and writes both marks on her slate with the care of a clerk recording a will contested.

choices.label: Carry both marks to the crow fields.

nodes.body: The flooded ford looks passable at the wrong hour, and you try it to save a single mile, and the current votes last and loudest. Gear soaked, tinder drowned, one pack gone spinning toward Dunmere. The party limps home to Barrowmere behind its own sneezes, and before anyone can hold a spoon steady again the season has shut like a door on the trail.

Under the third wain, where the chalk dust showed small footprints, you find a child's slate. Letters practiced in a careful row, TAM TAM TAM, and then one wobbly BERTA, schoolwork carried to market because pride travels. You wipe the mud from it with a thumb and pocket it like a writ. Some commissions are written on parchment with wax seals. This one is written on slate, in a child's hand, and it binds harder.

choices.label: Search on for whoever knocked.

nodes.body: The council hour is spent flat on the wain boards over Finch's map scrap, lantern hooded to a coin of light. Sable argues for the culvert, Bram argues the causeway like an honest soldier. Then Elowen asks what the wardens themselves would have used, and everyone stops. And looks at the drains again, and the map is quietly redrawn around a better question.

choices.label: Sleep on it and let the storm choose the camp.

nodes.body: You push the trail at moonrise against every counsel given, and misread the night sign within the mile. The hunting picket that watches the drag trail by dark folds around the party like a purse string drawing shut. The rescue joins the rescued, and the hills keep the lesson for whoever comes next, which now must be nobody.

A change horn sounds from the gatehouse, and the toll brazier post simply empties, both dicers trooping inside with their stakes abandoned mid game. The causeway stands suddenly bare as a swept floor, a hundred clear paces to the arch with nothing between but wind and firelight. Sable's eyebrows ask the question. Your legs answer it, and the company runs low and quiet up the empty stone.

choices.label: Take the empty causeway at a dead run.
Scoop the abandoned dice stakes as you pass.

nodes.body: You time the run to the horn's own echo, boots loud only in your own ears. The arch shadow takes you in just as the dicers troop back out, arguing about whose stakes are whose. Nobody looks down the causeway they just left empty. The party stands pressed into the dark of the gatehouse arch, one wall from the outer ward, richer by a crossing and nobody the wiser.

choices.label: Slip through the wicket into the ward.

nodes.body: The abandoned stakes prove too pretty to pass, weighted goblin bone with silver pips. Your palm has just closed on them when the dicers troop back early. Property is the one law every camp on earth shares. The argument is loud, short, and entirely one sided. It is conducted at spear point all the way to the cells, and the dice ride there in your pocket, confiscated twice before morning.

The cliff hollow hides a climber's cache, warden made by the knots, and you unwrap it like a message from the dead. Rope pegs, a rusted lantern hook, a coil of line that crumbles at one pull. But the pegs. The pegs are bronze, true as the day they were cast, and spaced exactly a body's length apart up the worst pitch, a ladder waiting a century for someone to remember it.

choices.label: Climb the peg line to the wall breach.
Trust the old warden rope up the last reach.

nodes.body: You trust the ancient rope for one reach too far, because it held the last three times, and a century of damp lets go all at once. The mountain finishes the argument the rope started. It is a short argument, and the heather has the last word, the way it has had the last word on this cliff since before the wardens came.

An otter run threads the culvert bank, low and dry and smelling of fish supper, wide enough for shoulders if pride stays outside. You send the packs through first and follow on elbows. The run bends twice, tight as a knot, and opens at last under the drain stairs inside the wall itself, an entrance no map of the keep has ever bothered to record. The otters, wherever they are, do not object.

choices.label: Take the otter run under the wall.
Push the pinch with your pack still on.

nodes.body: The pinch catches your pack straps exactly where an otter carries only appetite, and the run holds you like a cork in a bottle. Digging out backward by the ankles takes the rest of the night and most of your dignity. The wall watch grows curious about the grunting long before the end. The ditch is lit and lively by the time you come free, and the way in is finished for good.

Through a prop's width of dark you learn the dig crew's shift song, call and answer in mangled trade tongue, hauled bones and counted coins, verse after verse. Its rhythm is exactly the cover four sets of boots need. You cross the junction inside the song's own beat, step and pause and step, and the crew sings your crossing for you without ever knowing they had a choir.

choices.label: Cross the junction inside the songs beat.
Join the answer line to blend deeper.

nodes.body: You join the answer line one verse too boldly, and the call stops mid word. Lamplight swings toward harmony that nobody ordered. The junction becomes a chorus of one party and many picks, and the song the crew sings on the march back up is new, improvised, and about you. Goblin humor loses nothing in translation, and the cells have heard it all before.

You steal the water round whole, two buckets and a yoke from the laundry vats, and carry it down the cell row at a trusty's shuffle. Water ladles through the gratings into nine pairs of reaching hands while the warder dreams in his chair, and cup by cup the block maps itself. Who stands, who leans, which hinges creak. By the last grating you know the block better than its keepers do.

choices.label: Set the buckets down and move on the block.
Carry the yoke through the low lintel at speed.

nodes.body: The yoke catches the low lintel square, and two buckets ring down the cell block like temple gongs announcing a festival. The warder surfaces mid snore with his keys already swinging in his fist, reflexes older than his nap. The water round ends in irons, and the block listens to you being filed into it, cup by cup of bitter arithmetic.

The hall hound is gray at the muzzle and chained by the hearth, and it watches the party thread the sleepers with its ears up and its tail still. Too old to bark first and ask questions after, too wise to be fooled. You buy it one strip of jerky at a time, the last of the pouch, until its head goes back down on its paws. A toll paid to the only honest sentry in Harrowstone, and cheap at the price.

choices.label: Cross the last of the floor to the dais stair.
Stare the hound down and save the jerky.

nodes.body: You test the old hound with a stare instead of supper, and old dogs know exactly what stares mean. One bark, flat as a hammer on a board, and the windrows rise around you in waves. The hall howls you out through the midden door with nothing to show but a lesson in economics. The sentry was honest, and the toll was jerky, and you tried to pay in pride.

You corner the shaking novice softly in the niche shadow, one hand raised, no iron showing. It trades everything for a whispered promise that it can desert tonight. The shaman has scried the party since the moor. The den wards answer to bone, not steel. And the sigil hangs on a thong the shaman never knots, because who would dare. You let it slip away down the passage, one small deserter richer, and take the curtain edge with its secrets.

choices.label: Let it slip away and take the curtain edge.
Send the novice ahead to hold the curtain for you.

nodes.body: You trust the novice one favor further and send it ahead to hold the curtain. And it holds instead a whole whispered conversation with its master. The den empties toward you with the novice pointing, young, shaking, and sorry, which helps nobody in the cells. Some deserters need more of a head start than a promise, and some masters are worth more fear than freedom.

You fall in behind a train of plunder porters crossing the emptying floor, a crate on your shoulder for a pass and your chin tucked for a face. Malverin's own baggage train carries you toward the crypt stair in plain sight, which is the joke of it. The best shadows in Harrowstone turn out to be other people's burdens. You set the crate down at the stair head and step sideways into the dark.

choices.label: Set the crate down at the stair and slip aside.

nodes.body: A warden's spy slot sits at the postern's own hinge, and it frames the trestle close enough to count the reliquary's rivets. You learn the shaman's song breath by breath from here, the rise, the held note, the little falling turn. And you mark the exact half beat where the singer must inhale, a door left open in the music, wide enough for one moving shadow.

choices.label: Move on the rite at the half beat.

nodes.body: You count the lamp ring from the last pillar. Eleven lamps, two guttering, and the gap between the guttering pair is the width of a cart door. You rehearse the routes to the trestle twice behind closed eyes, every stride assigned its beat, before a single boot moves. Then the near lamp dips, the gap opens, and the rehearsal becomes the performance.

choices.label: Move on the rite through the guttering gap.
Cross while the near lamp gutters lowest.

nodes.body: One guttering lamp chooses that exact heartbeat to flare. The gap becomes a stage, and the crawl becomes a performance, reviewed at spear point by the nearest rank. The rite pauses politely while you are collected off the floor and dusted, and then resumes. Because nothing about you delays a door that has waited three hundred years.

You misjudge Vessk's patience by one demand too many, and the chief grows tired of humans who bargain like victors. His hand moves a finger's width. The honor guard closes with the peculiar gentleness goblins save for valuable hostages, and you spend the rite in the chief's own keeping, an asset now, awaiting valuation.

Your elbow splits the drum skin with one misjudged shift, and the flat note sits in the wall of right ones like a broken tooth. The captain's cord finds the flaw faster than thought can excuse it. The gallery box shuts its one door, and the night finishes without you in it, drummed out in every sense there is.

You ask Marta about her oldest lodger, and the room grows quieter without quite meaning to. Up the back stair, in a box bed beside a shuttered window, sits Sister Maren, blind and birch white and straight as a mast, the last of the warden order by her own account. Her dry hands find your face and read it like a page of scripture, and she pronounces you young and stubborn and going east, none of it a question. Marta hovers in the doorway, twisting her apron into knots.

choices.label: Ask her what the wardens actually warded.

nodes.body: Not monsters, child, she says in a voice dry as pressed flowers, but sleepers, for the deep places are full of old kings who did not die properly. A seal is not a lock, she tells you, it is a lullaby, and if the singing ever stops the sleeper stirs. Her grip closes on your wrist with terrible certainty. If someone is digging at Harrowstone, you go and you sing it back down. Then she releases you, satisfied, and mentions the dwarf by the fire downstairs. Take him, she says.

choices.label: Carry the lullaby word to the dwarf by the fire.

nodes.body: The lead wain's floor rings double under your knuckle, and Sable's eyebrows go up. A false bottom, the kind drovers build for the road purse, pried open on a shared look. Inside, untouched by raiders who never thought to knock, lie the crew's wages in a canvas roll and a bundle of letters home, sealed, stamped, and never sent. The wages weigh less than the letters do, and you take charge of both.

choices.label: Take charge of wages and letters both.

nodes.body: You count the letters against the guard roster, and it comes out exact. One letter for every name on the caravan, which means every name expected to come home and wrote ahead to say so. You pack them in oilcloth against the weather with the wages underneath. It is freight now, Bram says, watching you knot it, and freight gets delivered. The company files it under oath and turns back to the knocking.

choices.label: Go back to the knocking under the lead wain.

nodes.body: At the rail Weck presses a river charm on Elowen with both hands, braided eel skin around a drilled gray pebble, worn smooth by pockets before her pockets were born. Against the marsh, he says, not the river, the river and I are square. But if your road ever runs south into the Greymarsh, wear it, and do not trust the lights. Elowen thanks him properly. He grunts and looks away downriver, toward the marshes, and does not explain further.

choices.label: Stow the charm and follow the crows west.

nodes.body: You read the hedge bones by lantern shade, and they are not a hedge at all. Post holes in a great ring, the bones of an old droving corral, and where drovers penned cattle there is always water. The well sits sweet under its lid not ten paces on, and you fill every waterskin to bursting while the storm grumbles itself out overhead. And rise from the cold camp watered and rested for the drag trail.

choices.label: Rise watered and rested for the drag trail.

nodes.body: The lamed driver's satchel still hangs on the cage lashing where he left it. Inside, among fish hooks and loaded dice, lies a folded sheet of soft leather scratched with a route map. Not to the hills. South, causeway lines threading marsh country, mile on mile, down to a circle marked with a coiled snake. Pay dirt for somebody, someday, and you pocket the marsh map and go back to seeing to the boy.

choices.label: Pocket the marsh map and see to the boy.

nodes.body: A chain locker hides in the gatehouse footing, its portcullis gear a century seized into one red mass. But through the rusted grating a crawl space runs clean under the gate passage itself, and its floor glitters faintly. Dropped coins, years of them, fallen through the boards above into the one place in Harrowstone nobody could ever reach. Until somebody small enough, and poor enough, and here.

choices.label: Crawl the coin floor under the gate.

nodes.body: The crawl space gives onto the winch room stair inside the wall, and your pockets ride heavier by a fistful of strangers' coins, old mintings and older. The steps were built for chains to run on, smooth and shallow, and boots go quiet down them as if the stair approves of stealth. Below, the ward's firelight flickers through the door seams, close now.

choices.label: Take the winch stair down into the ward.

nodes.body: The old beacon basket hangs on its cliff bracket above the second pitch, iron rusted to black lace, but the bracket bolts still bite true stone. From its ledge a warden's sight line runs straight down the valley, and every approach to Harrowstone lies laid out below like a supper table. Watch fires, gate lamps, the breach, all of it plotted in one long look from a dead order's chair.

choices.label: Fix the watch fires positions from up here.

nodes.body: You commit the watch fires to memory with their blind lanes between, a map made of dark stripes on a dark hill. Two fires overlap at the breach, but their overlap wanders on the wind, and the wandering has a rhythm. You climb back down to the pitch knowing exactly where not to be seen, which is most of what stealth ever is.

choices.label: Slip the breach on the counted circuit.

nodes.body: A side chamber opens off the dry ledge, and the lantern makes it a room again for the first time in a century. A warden tally room, by the shelf stubs and the rotted ledgers gone to soil. But the wall itself is scribed deep, columns of years marching down the stone, and one entry repeating decade after decade in different hands. Sang the turning. All quiet. Then, farther down, the entries simply stop.

choices.label: Read the last legible year and move on.

nodes.body: The last legible entry is cut shallower than the rest, hurried, in a different hand than any of the years above it. No songs left, it reads. The door must hold. Below those words there is nothing but smoke stain and bare stone, a century of silence where the careful entries used to go. The lantern beam trembles slightly, and you tell yourself it is only because your hand does.

choices.label: Take the ledge on to the inner grate.

nodes.body: At Mag's nod you open the kiln heart itself, and the charge glows in there like a dragon's dream. Behind the heat shimmer stands her true door, a fired clay pipe tall as a dwarf, running straight into the hill, warm as an oven and dry as regret. Her grandmother's work, she says, holding the charge back with an iron paddle and centuries of practice. Quickly now. The kiln does not wait on courage.

choices.label: Duck through the kiln pipe while she holds the charge.

nodes.body: You walk the pipe at a crouch through bread scented dark, soot writing your biography on every surface it can reach. The warmth fades behind you a step at a time until the hill's own cold takes over. At the far end a vent grating lifts on stiff hinges, and you come up into the ward corner where the ovens once breathed. Singed, filthy, and inside the walls of Harrowstone.

choices.label: Lift the vent grating and slip into the ward.

nodes.body: The manifest board comes off the broken barrow, and Elowen reads it in lantern shade. Tallies of bones by the basket, level by level. The counts are wrong for a battlefield, she says slowly. No broken blades, no spear heads, no iron at all. Whole rows, whole families, laid down in order. The wardens buried their own around the thing they guarded, a hedge of the dead, and the diggers are pulling the hedge up root by root. You put the board back exactly as it lay.

choices.label: Put the board back exactly and take the chute.

nodes.body: Around the inner door the stone is scratched with a garrison's worth of boredom, initials and tallies and one rude drawing of a sergeant, a century of soldiers saying I was here. Among them, cut deeper and older than all of it, sits a warden's mark with an arrow pointing down, and two words in the old strokes. Choir stair. The building remembers its keepers, even if nothing else does, and it is still giving directions.

choices.label: Follow the old arrow down into the under keep.

nodes.body: Tallow gives you his whispered muster through the grate, the one he takes twice daily to keep heads counted and hearts up. Nine names in order of age, Berta the youngest, eleven and fierce. Two drovers fevered but walking. And Hobb, Tam's father, alive, his broken arm splinted with a soup spoon and profanity. You tell Tallow his freight is about to move, and the gray face behind the bars becomes, briefly, a much younger man's.

choices.label: Tell Tallow his freight is about to move.

nodes.body: The word passes cell to cell in the quiet game rhythm, tap and pause and tap, and the block answers it with a silence that has weight. Nine people pack nothing at all with tremendous care, straw smoothed, cloaks rolled, the child's slate wiped clean. By the time the keys come off the board the block is ready, and has been ready, you understand, since the first night the clapping started.

choices.label: Plan the out with the whole block ready.

nodes.body: Elowen bends over the wax tablet in the plunder shadow, and finds the margins working overtime. The fine hand keeps a second ledger in cipher between the lines, sums flowing to something called the Coil. Payment terms that read less like wages and more like tribute. Whoever Malverin serves, he pays them the way frightened men pay, early and in full, and he writes it down small.

choices.label: Copy the cipher lines onto your own slate.

nodes.body: You copy the cipher lines onto your own slate letter by meaningless letter, for wiser eyes than yours, Hedda's clerks or the abbey's. Elowen checks the copy twice against the wax. Then the tablet goes back into its straw nest exactly as it lay, unmissed. And the fresh lit passage waits beyond the plunder with its torches burning down toward moonset.

choices.label: Follow the passage toward the war court.

nodes.body: The crow spirit lingers past its debt out of pure corvid nosiness and offers gossip free of charge. The ash robed one smells of river mud under the ash, it says. The shaman scries him nightly and tells Vessk nothing. And below the door, the sleeper has started answering the seal one note late, like a singer losing the tune. It cocks its smoke head at you, delighted with itself, waiting to see what you do with any of it.

choices.label: Thank it and climb toward the gallery.

nodes.body: The spirit unfolds upward through the stone like smoke through cloth, gone between one blink and the next. One feather of actual matter is left turning on the floor where it stood, black with a white tip. You pocket it for no reason you could defend aloud, and Elowen pretends very hard not to have seen you do it, which is how you know she approves.

choices.label: Take the gallery stairs.

nodes.body: You count the wizard's pacing like a jailer's rounds. Eleven strides, turn. Eleven strides, turn. A scholar's discipline stretched over a debtor's nerves, and every eleventh stride his eyes flick to the processional stair, as if something might come up it early, asking after schedules. Whatever Malverin is, he is not the top of his own ladder. He is afraid of the next rung, and that is worth knowing tonight.

choices.label: Slip down toward the crypt approaches with that.

nodes.body: You study the last mural panel one breath longer, and the breath pays. Twelve wardens stand at the door, but one is painted turned away, hand over mouth. And under the flaking wash a scribed correction runs in the same old strokes. Eleven sang, and one listened, and the listener keeps the count. You carry that down the passage toward the blind side, and resolve to be the twelfth.

choices.label: Go to the blind side and keep the count yourself.

nodes.body: You shop the panic like a market day. A dropped torch collected here, a watchword learned from a shouting captain there. Spear racks passed at a porter's trudge and one goblin cloak lifted off its hook in passing. The confusion pays wages to anyone calm enough to collect them, and you walk toward the rite dressed in borrowed chaos, richer by every stride.

choices.label: Move on the rite dressed in borrowed chaos.

nodes.body: You count the rite time from inside the drum line, shoulder to shoulder with thunder. Nine beats and a rest, and nine and a rest. And in every rest the shaman's song stands alone and naked for half a breath, the exact seam in the night where a wrong note would tear the whole cloth. You carry the count down toward the floor like a key carried to its lock.

choices.label: Carry the count down to the rite.
